content.text: A few things surfaced that I want to understand before touching anything — origin/main was **force-pushed** (history rewritten), `git status` hit a **nested-repo error**, and `gh pr list` shows **no open PRs**. Let me dig before acting.

input.command:
cd /Users/aarushisahejpal/Dropbox/northwestern-challenge
echo "===== gh repo + PRs (explicit) ====="
gh repo view --json nameWithOwner,defaultBranchRef -q '.nameWithOwner, .defaultBranchRef.name' 2>&1
echo "--- open PRs ---"; gh pr list --repo aarushisahejpal/djnf-26 --state open 2>&1
echo "--- all recent PRs ---"; gh pr list --repo aarushisahejpal/djnf-26 --state all --limit 10 2>&1
echo
echo "===== local vs origin/main divergence ====="
git rev-list --left-right --count main...origin/main 2>&1
echo "(left=local-only, right=origin-only)"
echo
echo "===== the nested-repo error: submodules? ====="
cat .gitmodules 2>/dev/null || echo "(no .gitmodules)"
git config --file .gitmodules --list 2>/dev/null
echo "--- top-level tree ---"
ls -la | grep -vE '^total' | awk '{print $1, $NF}' | head -30
input.description: Check repo identity, PRs, divergence, submodules

content.text:
Now it's clear. Two colleagues: **Rob Calvey** (pushing directly to `main`) and **dmil** — that's Dhrumil, your collaborator — who opened the PRs. Three of them (#7/#8/#9) stack onto a `dev-dmil` branch; **only #4 targets `main`**:

> **PR #4 — `dev-dmil → main`:** *"Search by meaning, cite by keyword: a semantic discovery layer that feeds the audited lexicon maps (+2x faster corpus builds)"*

That's almost certainly the one to test and accept. Let me pull its details before I clone and test.

input.command:
R=aarushisahejpal/northwestern-ai-challenge
echo "===== PR #4 overview ====="
gh pr view 4 --repo $R --json title,author,additions,deletions,changedFiles,mergeable,mergeStateStatus,body \
  -q '"title: \(.title)\nauthor: \(.author.login)\nfiles: \(.changedFiles)  +\(.additions)/-\(.deletions)\nmergeable: \(.mergeable) / \(.mergeStateStatus)\n\n--- body ---\n\(.body)"' 2>&1 | head -60
echo
echo "===== CI checks on PR #4 (if any) ====="
gh pr checks 4 --repo $R 2>&1 | head -10
input.description: View PR #4 details and CI checks

content.content:
===== PR #4 overview =====
title: Search by meaning, cite by keyword: a semantic discovery layer that feeds the audited lexicon maps (+2x faster corpus builds)
author: dmil
files: 98  +56559/-55708
mergeable: MERGEABLE / CLEAN

--- body ---
📦 @aarushisahejpal — after merging, pull this 4.6 GB database (it has the embeddings in it):

```bash
wget https://dhrumil-public.s3.amazonaws.com/gain-investigation/lda_full_2026-07-14.duckdb -O db/lda_full.duckdb
sha256sum db/lda_full.duckdb   # 5f58461414a50ebd2c08d0d68dcdb090f9dd2272fe112617914bbd307fc42266
```

If you don't pull it, everything still builds from scratch — it just takes a while.

# Summary

Rolls up three components, each reviewed in its own PR — see those for full design notes, benchmarks, and verification evidence:

### 1. Parallelized corpus loading — #2
`build_db.py` fans press JSONL and House XML parsing out over a multiprocessing pool (`--workers`, default = CPU count). Full-corpus build drops **11m40s → 5m30s** with row-for-row identical output. Plus live progress in redirected logs (line-buffered stdout).

### 2. Semantic discovery layer — #3
A second discovery surface over the lobbying free-text, complementing the FTS/BM25 layer: `embed_corpus.py` (build side) embeds the 388K distinct activity-description texts into the DB with raw-record pointers preserved; `lda_semantic_search.py` (lead-scanner side) provides `--query` / `--like <record>` / `--compare-bm25`. Discovery-only per the repo's discovery/serving split — proposes vocabulary and leads; findings still cite the deterministic keyword chain. Production layer: `nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5`, 768-dim, built 2026-07-14.

### 3. Better crypto coverage — the new search layer's first real catch — #5
Our map of "who lobbies on crypto" is built from a fixed, versioned list of search phrases: a lobbying filing counts as crypto only if it literally contains one of them. That's great for trust — anyone can verify why a filing is on the list — but it misses filings that describe crypto in words the list doesn't have. The new meaning-based search proposed candidates, and each one was vetted before inclusion: its matching filings sampled and read in context, look-alike collisions hunted for, and the outcome — including rejections — recorded in the lexicon's changelog. Ten phrases survived the vetting:

> `nfts` · `non fungible token` / `non fungible tokens` · `non-fungible token` / `non-fungible tokens` · `digital collectible` / `digital collectibles` · `digital financial asset` / `digital financial assets`

The headline catch: **Yuga Labs — the Bored Ape Yacht Club company — had been lobbying Washington about "non fungible tokens and digital collectibles" for years and never appeared on our crypto map**, because none of our phrases mentioned NFTs. It now shows up as what it is: a company whose lobbying is 100% crypto, with $625K in total lobbying spend.

Two checks worth knowing about: we considered adding the bare word `nft` but rejected it — it turns out there's an unrelated draft bill called the "New Frontier in Technology (NFT) Act" that would have polluted the list. And the update was verified non-destructive: all 9,768 previously-identified crypto filings are still there (61 new ones joined), and no other topic's numbers moved at all. The crypto dashboard was regenerated, and now includes a card showing the full phrase list — including the words we deliberately rejected and why — so a reader can see exactly what "crypto" means on that page.

===== PR #4 overview =====
title: Search by meaning, cite by keyword: a semantic discovery layer that feeds the audited lexicon maps (+2x faster corpus builds)
author: dmil
files: 98  +56559/-55708
mergeable: MERGEABLE / CLEAN

--- body ---
📦 @aarushisahejpal — after merging, pull this 4.6 GB database (it has the embeddings in it):

```bash
wget https://dhrumil-public.s3.amazonaws.com/gain-investigation/lda_full_2026-07-14.duckdb -O db/lda_full.duckdb
sha256sum db/lda_full.duckdb   # 5f58461414a50ebd2c08d0d68dcdb090f9dd2272fe112617914bbd307fc42266
```

If you don't pull it, everything still builds from scratch — it just takes a while.

# Summary

Rolls up three components, each reviewed in its own PR — see those for full design notes, benchmarks, and verification evidence:

### 1. Parallelized corpus loading — #2
`build_db.py` fans press JSONL and House XML parsing out over a multiprocessing pool (`--workers`, default = CPU count). Full-corpus build drops **11m40s → 5m30s** with row-for-row identical output. Plus live progress in redirected logs (line-buffered stdout).

### 2. Semantic discovery layer — #3
A second discovery surface over the lobbying free-text, complementing the FTS/BM25 layer: `embed_corpus.py` (build side) embeds the 388K distinct activity-description texts into the DB with raw-record pointers preserved; `lda_semantic_search.py` (lead-scanner side) provides `--query` / `--like <record>` / `--compare-bm25`. Discovery-only per the repo's discovery/serving split — proposes vocabulary and leads; findings still cite the deterministic keyword chain. Production layer: `nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5`, 768-dim, built 2026-07-14.

### 3. Better crypto coverage — the new search layer's first real catch — #5
Our map of "who lobbies on crypto" is built from a fixed, versioned list of search phrases: a lobbying filing counts as crypto only if it literally contains one of them. That's great for trust — anyone can verify why a filing is on the list — but it misses filings that describe crypto in words the list doesn't have. The new meaning-based search proposed candidates, and each one was vetted before inclusion: its matching filings sampled and read in context, look-alike collisions hunted for, and the outcome — including rejections — recorded in the lexicon's changelog. Ten phrases survived the vetting:

> `nfts` · `non fungible token` / `non fungible tokens` · `non-fungible token` / `non-fungible tokens` · `digital collectible` / `digital collectibles` · `digital financial asset` / `digital financial assets`

The headline catch: **Yuga Labs — the Bored Ape Yacht Club company — had been lobbying Washington about "non fungible tokens and digital collectibles" for years and never appeared on our crypto map**, because none of our phrases mentioned NFTs. It now shows up as what it is: a company whose lobbying is 100% crypto, with $625K in total lobbying spend.

Two checks worth knowing about: we considered adding the bare word `nft` but rejected it — it turns out there's an unrelated draft bill called the "New Frontier in Technology (NFT) Act" that would have polluted the list. And the update was verified non-destructive: all 9,768 previously-identified crypto filings are still there (61 new ones joined), and no other topic's numbers moved at all. The crypto dashboard was regenerated, and now includes a card showing the full phrase list — including the words we deliberately rejected and why — so a reader can see exactly what "crypto" means on that page.

README.md
# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — Submission

> **Status: SKELETON.** This README is the submission map required by the challenge. Sections below
> match the six required elements verbatim; they are filled in as the work happens, not reconstructed
> at the deadline.

## What this is

An agentic investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional
press releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. Built as re-runnable Agent
Skills plus verified findings.

## Quick start (evaluators)

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --sample 2025-Q1   # smoke mode
python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>                    # view any cited record
```

Data layout under `data/` follows the challenge's data manual (`congress_press/`, `senate/`, `house/`).

## 1. Included skills

| Skill | What it does | How to run |
|---|---|---|
| `skills/lda-corpus-loader/` | Parses all three raw datasets into a queryable DB; every row keeps a raw-record pointer; includes `show_record.py` | `python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years YYYY ...]` |
| `skills/lda-entity-resolver/` | Cross-dataset entity table + alias map (Senate↔House crosswalk); resolves the `<senateID>` compound key, groups clients by normalized name (Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global) | `python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/resolve_entities.py --db db/lda.duckdb [--report]` |
| `skills/investigation-ledger/` | Lead/entity/thread tracking templates + schema lint | `python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md` |
| `skills/lead-scanner/` | (1) Lens library: SQL-first anomaly scans that emit candidate leads. (2) Bill cross-check: a bill number **or** named alias → every Senate/House filing + press release touching it. (3) LD-203 giving map: an entity/roster → its disclosed political giving. (4) Industry map: an industry hidden in the lobbying free-text → an entity-resolved player roster. (5) FEC enrichment: that roster → its Super-PAC contributions (openFEC), reconciled against LD-203 (the money LD-203 can't see) | Lenses: `python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX] [queries/<file>.sql]`. Bill: `python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Inflation Reduction Act"`. Giving: `lda_ld203_giving.py --names-file <roster>`. Industry: `lda_industry_map.py crypto`. FEC: `fec_enrich.py --names-file out/crypto_roster.txt` (needs `DATA_GOV_API_KEY`) |
| `skills/finding-verifier/` | Fresh-agent re-derivation of every claim in a finding before it locks | Protocol in `SKILL.md`, not yet automated end-to-end; delegates outside-source PDFs to `skills/source-document-reader` |
| `skills/source-document-reader/` | Turns an external primary-source PDF (court filing, SEC complaint) into page-anchored, citable text via render-to-image OCR that survives broken e-filing fonts; defines the external-document citation convention | `python skills/source-document-reader/scripts/ocr_pdf.py --pdf data/DOC.pdf --out data/DOC.ocr.txt [--grep TERM] [--dpi 300]` (needs the Tesseract engine — see `SKILL.md`) |
| `skills/outside-context-scan/` | Exploratory web research (novelty/prior-art + news-landscape checks) to triage a lead before drafting — distinct from finding-verifier's claim-locking rigor | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script |
| `skills/dataset-primer/` | Orients on an unfamiliar dataset before building against it: a bounded five-axis web scan (authoritative docs, tribal-knowledge gotchas, tooling, derived datasets, access mechanics) against a fixed nine-category data-quality checklist → a tiered, task-tailored reference brief cached in `reference/`. General-purpose research aid; sibling to outside-context-scan | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script. Example output: `reference/fec-campaign-finance.md` |

## 2. Which findings each skill supports

_Filled in as findings lock. See `findings/` (one file per locked finding)._

| Finding | Skills used | Trace files |

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Smoke test: build the DB from tests/fixtures/data (tiny excerpts of real public
records) and verify table counts, citation round-trips, and the ledger lint.

Run:  .venv/Scripts/python tests/smoke_test.py     (or any python with duckdb+ijson)
"""

import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path

REPO = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]
PY = sys.executable
FIX = REPO / "tests" / "fixtures" / "data"
DB = REPO / "tests" / "tmp" / "lda_test.duckdb"


def run(*args):
    r = subprocess.run([PY, *map(str, args)], capture_output=True, text=True,
                       cwd=REPO, encoding="utf-8")
    if r.returncode != 0:
        print(r.stdout)
        print(r.stderr)
        sys.exit(f"FAILED: {' '.join(map(str, args))}")
    return r.stdout


def main():
    DB.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    if DB.exists():

content.content:
===== semantic search: help + no-embeddings behavior =====
usage: lda_semantic_search.py [-h] [--db DB] [--query QUERY]
                              [--like RECORD_KEY] [-k K] [--compare-bm25]

Semantic (embedding) discovery search over lobbying free-text.

DISCOVERY ONLY — same posture as the FTS/BM25 layer this complements: results
propose vocabulary (for industry_lexicon.json) and leads; findings cite records
via the deterministic keyword serving tables, never via embedding similarity.
Every hit resolves to raw filings via show_record.py <record_key>.

Reads the tables embed_corpus.py builds (lobbying_text_embeddings +
lobbying_text_map). Search runs entirely in DuckDB (brute-force cosine over
FLOAT[dim] arrays — sub-second at 388K vectors); the embedding model is only
loaded to embed --query text, so --like needs no model at all.

Usage:
  python lda_semantic_search.py --query "pharmacy middlemen taking a cut of drug prices"
  python lda_semantic_search.py --query "..." --compare-bm25       # side-by-side vs FTS
  python lda_semantic_search.py --like <filing_uuid|house_id>      # neighbors of a filing
  python lda_semantic_search.py --query "..." -k 25 --db db/lda_full.duckdb

# Drop-in local replacement for lobbyR::get_filings() that reads the pre-downloaded
# Senate LDA JSON in data/senate/{year}/filings/filings_{year}.json instead of
# calling the live lda.gov API. The local JSON is the same shape as the API's
# `results` array (verified against get_filings.R's httr2::resp_body_json(...,
# simplifyVector = TRUE, flatten = TRUE) call), so this loader reproduces the
# exact tidy output shape get_filings() returns -- flag_dupes() and
# flag_client_registrant_conflict() (scripts/lobbyr_clean.R) run on it unmodified.
#
# Unlike get_filings(), which is capped at one `year` per API call, `years` here
# accepts a vector so a single call can span the whole 2022-2026 Q1 corpus.

#' Load local Senate LDA quarterly filings
#'
#' @param years Numeric vector of filing years to load, e.g. 2022:2026.
#' @param data_root Path to the senate data directory. Default "data/senate".
#' @param issues Optional character vector of keywords to search for in lobbying
#'   activity descriptions (case-insensitive substring match).
#' @param issue_joiner "and" or "or" -- how multiple `issues` terms combine. Required if length(issues) > 1.
#' @param filing_period Optional exact match against the dataset's filing_period
#'   values: "first_quarter", "second_quarter", "third_quarter", "fourth_quarter"
#'   (also accepts "mid_year", "year_end" for older filings).
#' @param client_name Optional case-insensitive substring match against client name.
#' @param registrant_name Optional case-insensitive substring match against registrant name.
#' @param starting_date,ending_date Optional "YYYY-MM-DD" strings bounding `dt_posted`.
#' @param min_amount,max_amount Optional numeric bounds. Matches if EITHER income
#'   or expenses falls in range (the raw filing reports whichever applies to that
#'   registrant type) -- a simplification of the API's single "amount reported" filter.
#' @param tidy_result Logical (default TRUE). If TRUE, reduces to key columns
#'   (same whitelist as get_filings() plus the two carry-through columns below).
#' @param ignore_disclaimer Logical (default FALSE). Suppresses the double-counting disclaimer message.
---- R deps present? ----
dplyr TRUE 
jsonlite TRUE 
purrr TRUE 
tidyr TRUE 
stringr TRUE
Shell cwd was reset to /Users/aarushisahejpal/Dropbox/northwestern-challenge

input.description: Find documented build pipeline order

content.content:
===== documented pipeline (CLAUDE.md) =====
51:   `v_client_canonical_spend`; its P6 member layer (`build_members.py`) adds `members_all` /
52-   `member_terms` / `member_committees` from external sources (congress-legislators + FEC
53-   committee files), and `member_resolve.py` is the shared person/committee resolver the
54-   giving map's member rollup imports.
55:   - Then `lead-scanner`'s `lda_industry_map.py --build-tags` writes the `lobbying_issue_mentions`
56-     serving table — a research-driven enrichment, so it runs here, after the two above.
57-
58-**Investigate the corpus** (read-only over the DB + outside sources; run anytime after the build):
--
79:.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py \
80-    --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years 2025 2026] [--sample 2025-Q1]
81-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>
82-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/resolve_entities.py --db db/lda_full.duckdb  # entities/aliases/crosswalk (+ --report)
83:.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/build_members.py --db db/lda_full.duckdb  # members_all/member_terms/member_committees (P6; external sources, cached to out/)
84-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/member_resolve.py "Emmer for Congress"  # resolve a filed person/committee string (+ --date --json)
85:.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/backfill_press_issues.py --db db/lda_full.duckdb  # (re)build press_issue_mentions in place
86-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/add_lobbying_freetext.py --db db/lda_full.duckdb  # build lobbying_freetext + FTS in place
87-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/embed_corpus.py --db db/lda_full.duckdb  # build the semantic layer in place (optional deps: torch/sentence-transformers/pyarrow)
88-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_semantic_search.py --query "..." [--compare-bm25] [--like <key>]  # semantic discovery search
89:.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_industry_map.py --build-tags  # (re)build lobbying_issue_mentions from industry_lexicon.json
90-.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_turnover.py [2025Q4]  # quarterly turnover beat: terminations/hires/swaps/in-house (+ --json)
91-.venv/Scripts/python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda_full.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX]
92-.venv/Scripts/python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md
--
100:`skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py`):
101-- `db/lda_full.duckdb` — 2022–2026, all years. **Canonical/primary as of 2026-07-06.**
102-  Start new investigative work here.
103-- `db/lda_pilot.duckdb` — 2025 + 2026-Q1 only. Kept solely to reproduce
--
129:- `press_issue_mentions` — press releases tagged to ALI issue codes via the curated `ISSUE_KEYWORDS`
130:  dict in `build_db.py` (rebuilt in place by `backfill_press_issues.py`); ~80% of releases carry ≥1 tag.
131-- `lobbying_freetext` (+ FTS index) — Senate activity descriptions + House `specific_issues` unioned
132-  into one BM25-searchable doc surface, each row keeping a `show_record.py` `record_key`. The
133:  loader-owned, vocabulary-free **search/discovery** layer (P4); built in `build_db.py`'s index step,
134-  rebuilt in place by `add_lobbying_freetext.py`. FTS uses `stemmer='none'` and is discovery-only.
135:- `lobbying_issue_mentions` — lobbying free-text tagged to **industry facets** (e.g. `CRYPTO`) via the
136-  curated `skills/lead-scanner/scripts/industry_lexicon.json`; the deterministic, cited **serving**
137:  table (mirror of `press_issue_mentions`, on the lobbying side). Built by `lda_industry_map.py --build-tags`;
138-  feeds the entity-resolved industry player list (P4). Discovery (FTS/keyness) proposes vocabulary;

file.content:
# ---------------------------------------------------------------- test 2
def test_lexicon_guard():
    print("\n== 2. lexicon v1.2 guard — CRYPTO/PARDONS slices frozen")
    try:
        import duckdb
    except ImportError:
        check("duckdb importable", False)
        return
    db = REPO / "db" / "lda_full.duckdb"
    if not db.exists():
        print("  [skip] db/lda_full.duckdb not present")
        return
    con = duckdb.connect(str(db), read_only=True)
    for tag, want_rows, want_filings in (("CRYPTO", 30922, 9768), ("PARDONS", 576, 366)):
        n, f = con.execute(
            "SELECT count(*), count(DISTINCT record_key) FROM lobbying_issue_mentions "
            "WHERE tag=?", [tag]).fetchone()
        check(f"{tag} slice frozen", (n, f) == (want_rows, want_filings),
              f"rows={n} filings={f} (want {want_rows}/{want_filings})")
    n = con.execute("SELECT count(DISTINCT record_key) FROM lobbying_issue_mentions "
                    "WHERE tag='CRITMIN'").fetchone()[0]
    check("CRITMIN slice exists", n > 10000, f"{n} filings")
    con.close()

content.text:
Stopping here — nothing more gets added until you say so. Full transparency on where things stand:

## What I've already done (one push happened before your message)
1. **Tested PR #4 thoroughly** — all on a scratch clone, not the real repo:
   - Smoke test ✅ · parallel full-corpus build ✅ (141k press / 418k Senate / 410k House → 2.3 GB DB) · documented pipeline (entities → members → industry tags) ✅ · semantic CLI degrades gracefully without embeddings ✅
   - **pkg_acceptance:** failed only on the CRYPTO frozen-guard constant — the PR shipped lexicon v1.4 but the test still froze v1.2 numbers. Our fresh build produced **exactly** the PR's documented claim (9,768 + 61 = 9,829), so the test constant was the broken part.
   - **p6_acceptance:** the "5 unexplained changes" failure is **pre-existing on `main` too** (A/B tested) — it's upstream FEC/legislator data drift, **not the PR**. Left completely alone.
2. **The one thing I pushed:** a 1-line fixup to `dev-dmil` updating the guard constants to the PR's own verified numbers (`ebc0659..180b858`). All checks now pass. That's the only change to anyone's work, made under the "only if broken" rule — but if you'd rather Dhrumil make that call, I can revert it with one command and leave a PR comment instead.

## What is NOT done — awaiting your go-ahead
- **Merging PR #4 into `main`** — ready, mergeable/clean, tests green.
- **Importing Chris's work** — staged but not committed anywhere. Planned: `dashboard_app.R`, `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` (his lobbyR port — tested working: 409 stablecoin filings → 357 deduped → 331 conflict-cleaned), `data_manual.md`, `old_file_ignore/`, `.vscode/`; **excluding** `skills/lobbying-issue-theme-clustering/` (text analysis) and his root `.gitignore`/`.gitattributes` (would overwrite the team's).
- Note: PRs **#7/#8/#9** (Dhrumil's lexicon harvests v1.5–v1.7) target `dev-dmil`, not `main` — they'd stay open after #4 merges. Combining "everything" arguably includes them too.

Say the word on each: (a) keep or revert my guard fixup, (b) merge #4, (c) proceed with Chris's import as scoped, (d) what to do with #7/8/9.

# Lobbying quarterly filings

Adapts the `lobbyR` R package (github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr, built against the live
`lda.gov` API) to run entirely against the pre-downloaded corpus in `data/senate/`. Scope is
deliberately limited to the **Senate quarterly activity filings** (`data/senate/*/filings/`)
-- not House data, not the LD-203 contribution reports, not registrations-only filings.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — Submission

> **Status: SKELETON.** This README is the submission map required by the challenge. Sections below
> match the six required elements verbatim; they are filled in as the work happens, not reconstructed
> at the deadline.

## What this is

An agentic investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional
press releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. Built as re-runnable Agent
Skills plus verified findings.

## Quick start (evaluators)

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --sample 2025-Q1   # smoke mode
python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>                    # view any cited record
```

Data layout under `data/` follows the challenge's data manual (`congress_press/`, `senate/`, `house/`).

## 1. Included skills

| Skill | What it does | How to run |
|---|---|---|
| `skills/lda-corpus-loader/` | Parses all three raw datasets into a queryable DB; every row keeps a raw-record pointer; includes `show_record.py` | `python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years YYYY ...]` |
| `skills/lda-entity-resolver/` | Cross-dataset entity table + alias map (Senate↔House crosswalk); resolves the `<senateID>` compound key, groups clients by normalized name (Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global) | `python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/resolve_entities.py --db db/lda.duckdb [--report]` |
| `skills/investigation-ledger/` | Lead/entity/thread tracking templates + schema lint | `python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md` |
| `skills/lead-scanner/` | (1) Lens library: SQL-first anomaly scans that emit candidate leads. (2) Bill cross-check: a bill number **or** named alias → every Senate/House filing + press release touching it. (3) LD-203 giving map: an entity/roster → its disclosed political giving. (4) Industry map: an industry hidden in the lobbying free-text → an entity-resolved player roster. (5) FEC enrichment: that roster → its Super-PAC contributions (openFEC), reconciled against LD-203 (the money LD-203 can't see) | Lenses: `python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX] [queries/<file>.sql]`. Bill: `python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Inflation Reduction Act"`. Giving: `lda_ld203_giving.py --names-file <roster>`. Industry: `lda_industry_map.py crypto`. FEC: `fec_enrich.py --names-file out/crypto_roster.txt` (needs `DATA_GOV_API_KEY`) |
| `skills/finding-verifier/` | Fresh-agent re-derivation of every claim in a finding before it locks | Protocol in `SKILL.md`, not yet automated end-to-end; delegates outside-source PDFs to `skills/source-document-reader` |
| `skills/source-document-reader/` | Turns an external primary-source PDF (court filing, SEC complaint) into page-anchored, citable text via render-to-image OCR that survives broken e-filing fonts; defines the external-document citation convention | `python skills/source-document-reader/scripts/ocr_pdf.py --pdf data/DOC.pdf --out data/DOC.ocr.txt [--grep TERM] [--dpi 300]` (needs the Tesseract engine — see `SKILL.md`) |
| `skills/outside-context-scan/` | Exploratory web research (novelty/prior-art + news-landscape checks) to triage a lead before drafting — distinct from finding-verifier's claim-locking rigor | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script |
| `skills/dataset-primer/` | Orients on an unfamiliar dataset before building against it: a bounded five-axis web scan (authoritative docs, tribal-knowledge gotchas, tooling, derived datasets, access mechanics) against a fixed nine-category data-quality checklist → a tiered, task-tailored reference brief cached in `reference/`. General-purpose research aid; sibling to outside-context-scan | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script. Example output: `reference/fec-campaign-finance.md` |

## 2. Which findings each skill supports

_Filled in as findings lock. See `findings/` (one file per locked finding)._

| Finding | Skills used | Trace files |
|---|---|---|
| — | — | — |

## 3. Where the relevant traces are

`traces/` — one JSONL per session, named `YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead-id>.jsonl`.
`traces/INDEX.md` maps finding → skill invocations → trace files → human decisions.
`traces/rendered/` — HTML renderings for fast review.
Human-judgment moments are logged in `DECISIONS.md` and cross-referenced from the index.

## 4. Outside data used

_Disclosed as used. Candidates: Congress.gov (bills/votes/committees), FEC (campaign finance),
FARA (foreign agents), Federal Register._

| Source | Used for | Where cited |
|---|---|---|
| openFEC API (`api.open.fec.gov/v1`, api.data.gov key; fetched 2026-07-07) — endpoints `/committees` (committee resolution), `/schedules/schedule_a` (itemized receipts), `/committee/{id}/totals` (published-total reconciliation) | L031 (crypto Enterprise-Map money leg): each crypto player's contributions into the Fairshake Super-PAC network (committees `C00835959` Fairshake, `C00836221` Defend American Jobs, `C00848440` Protect Progress), reconciled against LD-203 giving — the Super-PAC money LD-203 can't see | `skills/lead-scanner/fec_enrich.py` (SKILL.md "FEC enrichment"); LEDGER L031 row; raw responses cached in `out/fec_cache/` (gitignored; the cache + committee/transaction ids are the citeable form — FEC data is external, not in the DB). API key read from env/gitignored keyfile — never committed or traced |
| unitedstates/congress-legislators (`legislators-current.json` + `legislators-historical.json`, unitedstates.github.io, public domain CC0; fetched 2026-07-09) | Member resolution layer (P6): `members_all`/`member_terms` — every member serving 2021+, name parts/nicknames, party per term with dates, FEC candidate ids | `skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/build_members.py`; raw JSON cached in `out/congress_legislators_cache/` (gitignored) |
| FEC bulk committee files (`cm`/`ccl`/`cn` per cycle 2022/2024/2026, fec.gov bulk-downloads; fetched 2026-07-09) + openFEC `/committees?designation=D` (leadership-PAC sponsor ids; key from env, never committed) | Candidate-support committee crosswalk (P6): `member_committees` — campaign committees / leadership PACs / JFC participants, tier-labeled | `skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/build_members.py`; raw files cached in `out/fec_cache/bulk/` + `out/fec_cache/leadership_pacs/` (gitignored) |
| FARA bulk data — `FARA_All_ForeignPrincipals.csv` (efile.fara.gov/bulk, fetched 2026-07-06, updated daily by DOJ) | L006 (UAE sovereign wealth): confirming zero FARA registrations for the Mubadala family and that ADIA's six historical registrations all terminated by 2022 | `archive/LEDGER_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md` L006 row (pilot-scale ledger, archived 2026-07-06 — see CLAUDE.md); `queries/l006_mubadala.sql` header |
| SEC Litigation Release 26458 + complaint PDF; Tracxn team profile; innovairrs.com | L003 (Innovairrs identity link) — lead work, parked; allegations-only framing mandatory | `archive/LEDGER_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md` L003 row + Cold threads (pilot-scale ledger, archived 2026-07-06 — see CLAUDE.md) |

## 5. Conflicts of interest

_One statement per team member — connections (employment, financial, family, prior work) to any
person or organization named in the findings, or "none known against the entities named."_

| Team member | Statement |
|---|---|
| — | — |

## 6. Possible legal violations flagged

_Any finding whose `Legal flag:` field is not "none" is listed here for the evaluation panel._

| Finding | Flag | Provision |
|---|---|---|
| — | — | — |

---
name: project_gain_challenge
description: GAIN Northwestern agentic-AI investigative journalism challenge; say-vs-pay Agent Skill
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 7a7f7c2e-8147-4b58-ae29-500d7b0ea3d1
---

Aarushi is competing in the GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge (Northwestern),
building Claude Code **Agent Skills** over a federal corpus (2022–2026 Q1):
Senate LDA JSON, House LDA XML, and congressional press releases. Judged on
findings quality (gate) + four 0–3 axes: Organization, Corpus Efficiency, Human
Verifiability, Novel Capabilities; reproducibility is a gate (can't re-run → cap
1). Deadline was July 15 2026; prizes $5k/$2.5k/$1k. Multiple skills allowed;
skills must be MIT + open source, validate against agentskills.io/specification.

**Personal tinker repo (separate from team repo `djnf-26`):**
https://github.com/aarushisahejpal/say-vs-pay (private, `personal` remote),
local at `~/Dropbox/say-vs-pay`. Corpus lives at
`~/Dropbox/northwestern-challenge/data.zip` (+ `data_manual.md`).

**Built skill `say-vs-pay`:** semantic vector index (chromadb) over
lobbying-activity descriptions + passage-level press text; tools
extract_lobbying/extract_press/build_index/query/say_vs_pay/map_semantics/setup.
say_vs_pay surfaces cited "say vs pay" candidate pairs (bi-encoder retrieve →
optional cross-encoder rerank → bill/chamber tie-break) for the agent to
adjudicate — never auto-declares contradictions. Precision fixes (passage
embedding + cross-encoder) killed the false positives found in testing.

**Ease/size architecture (as of this session):** `--backend auto` = use
sentence-transformers(+MPS, ~2370 txt/s) if torch installed, else Chroma ONNX
MiniLM (no torch, ~350MB base install, portable but ~45-slow on CPU). Phrase
DEDUP before embedding = 3.7x fewer embeddings (1.29M→350k unique). setup.py =
one-command extract+build; `--zip` brings own data; `--demo` builds tiny bundled
sample (assets/*.jsonl, 2.4MB) so anyone can install+explore in ~1min with NO
corpus/torch (PROVEN via fresh clone). paths.py auto-resolves --index via
.saypay.json (gitignored). Default indexes recent years (2025-26); --all-years
for full. Cross-encoder (rerank.py) is OPTIONAL (needs torch), degrades to
bi-encoder. IMPORTANT: USE_TF=0 baked into embedding.py/rerank.py avoids
TensorFlow AVX crash (exit 134). Corpus extracted at ~/nw-build/data (8.6G).

**Collaborator:** Dhrumil Mehta (co-authors semantic mapping with Aarushi) —
credited in LICENSE/README/SKILL.md. Planned companion skill `lda-tidy` ports
the **lobbyR** R package (github.com/cran/lobbyR, author Chris Cioffi; Aarushi
contributed) data-quality checks flag_dupes / flag_client_registrant_conflict —
disclose as COI. See [[feedback_no_fabrication]] [[feedback_flag_fuzzy_matches]].

**Next:** build lda-tidy; scale extraction to full corpus + rebuild index;
produce findings/ + traces/ (verify leads at source before asserting).

content.text:
Done — everything combined, tested, and pushed. Summary:

## What's on `main` now (`aarushisahejpal/northwestern-ai-challenge` @ `d8b5265`)
- **Rob's work** — untouched, as the gold-standard structure.
- **Dhrumil's PR #4** — merged after full testing: parallel loader (verified with a from-scratch full-corpus build: 141k press / 418k Senate / 410k House → 2.3 GB DB), semantic discovery layer (degrades gracefully without embeddings), lexicon v1.4 with the NFT/Yuga Labs catch. His work extends Rob's skills in place — no structural conflict.
- **Chris's work** — imported additively: `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` (his lobbyR port — tested from repo root: 409 stablecoin filings → 331 after his dedup/conflict cleaning), `dashboard_app.R`, `data_manual.md`, legacy files under `archive/chris-lobbyr-legacy/`, README table row added. **Excluded:** the text-analysis clustering skill (per team decision — the dashboard's clustering tab shows its built-in graceful notice).

## Changes made to others' work (only where broken)
1. **One test constant** on Dhrumil's branch: the CRYPTO guard froze v1.2 numbers while his PR shipped v1.4 — updated to his own verified numbers (9,829 = 9,768 + 61). Everything else as-authored.
2. Dropped a 45 MB `.cache` file **my test run generated** (Chris's own repo gitignores it) and mirrored his ignore rule.

## Final state: smoke PASS · pkg ALL PASS · Chris's R skill OK

## Loose ends for the team
- **PRs #7/#8/#9** (Dhrumil's lexicon harvests v1.5–v1.7) still target `dev-dmil`, not `main` — same treatment when you're ready.
- **Optional 4.6 GB embeddings DB** — Dhrumil's PR note: `wget https://dhrumil-public.s3.amazonaws.com/gain-investigation/lda_full_2026-07-14.duckdb` to skip local embedding.
- **Known-not-a-bug:** p6's retrofit regression fails identically on pre-merge `main` — upstream FEC/legislator data drifted since the audit CSVs froze (e.g., Wesley Bell $5k→$10k). Worth Dhrumil re-freezing those CSVs when convenient.
- **The real remaining gap for the deadline (July 15!):** the findings docs — the README's findings/skills mapping table is still skeleton.

message.content: dhrumil's thing fit well into the rob's skills ?

content.text:
Yes — cleanly, and it's verifiable in the merged diff. His PR created **zero new skill directories**; every change landed *inside* skills Rob's structure already had:

| Where it landed | What Dhrumil added |
|---|---|
| `skills/lda-corpus-loader/` | Parallelized `build_db.py` (`--workers`) + new `embed_corpus.py`, SKILL.md updated in place |
| `skills/lead-scanner/` | New `lda_semantic_search.py` + `industry_lexicon.json` bumped to v1.4 (the NFT phrases), SKILL.md updated |
| `skills/industry-review-packager/` | Small refinements |
| `out/packages/` (82 of the 98 changed files) | Rob's dashboard packages **regenerated** through Rob's own packaging system with the new lexicon — not replaced |

Just as important, he followed the repo's existing *design rules*, not just its folders: the semantic layer is explicitly **discovery-only** ("findings cite records via the deterministic keyword serving tables, never via embedding similarity"), which is Rob's discovery/serving split applied to the new capability. And the proof it integrated rather than collided: after merging, Rob's own test suite passes on the combined `main`, and the crypto slice change was surgical — all 9,768 prior filings retained, 61 added, no other topic's numbers moved.

The one seam I had to touch was the stale test constant (his lexicon update vs. the frozen guard numbers) — a bookkeeping miss, not a structural conflict.

So: Rob's structure absorbed Dhrumil's work as deepening, Chris's as a side-by-side addition. That's the right shape for the submission.

message.content: okay and then chris's compliments?

lastPrompt: okay and then chris's compliments?

content.text:
Yes — but differently than Dhrumil's. His *deepened* Rob's skills; Chris's **complements them from outside**, filling gaps the Python/DuckDB stack doesn't cover:

**1. The double-counting discipline (his real unique contribution).** `flag_dupes()` + `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` are explicit, documented cleaning steps that stop amendments/duplicates and registrant==client filings from inflating spend totals. We measured it live: 2025 stablecoin filings went **409 → 357 → 331** — roughly 1 in 5 naive rows would have double-counted. Rob's stack resolves *entities*; nobody else in the repo does this *filing-level* spend hygiene as an auditable step.

**2. An independent second computation path.** Chris's R skill and the DuckDB pipeline read the **same raw JSON** through completely separate implementations. For a challenge scored on verifiability, that's genuinely valuable: any headline spend number can be cross-checked against an independent codebase — "two implementations agree" is a much stronger claim than "our pipeline says so."

**3. A different explorer for a different user.** Rob's `out/packages` are static, citable review dashboards; Chris's Shiny app is an *interactive* query UI — issue/client/registrant/period filters, no SQL — that an R-fluent reporter can drive directly. Different consumption mode, no overlap in function.

**4. Lineage that strengthens the submission.** It ports **lobbyR**, a published package — real provenance for the methodology. (One to-do that raises: the submission README should disclose that you contributed to lobbyR, per the challenge's conflict-of-interest requirement.)

**Honest overlaps/limits:** its keyword filtering and top-spender rollups duplicate things the FTS layer and `v_client_canonical_spend` already do, and it's Senate-quarterlies only — no House XML, no LD-203, no press. So it's the narrowest of the three bodies of work — but since the submission is "a bunch of skills," a self-contained, spec-valid skill with one distinctive method is exactly the right shape.

One thought worth taking to the team: the strongest way to *use* the complement is a paragraph in the findings docs showing a key spend total computed both ways (DuckDB and Chris's R pipeline) landing on the same number after his dedup — that turns the redundancy into a reproducibility argument for the judges.

pipeline (`lda-corpus-loader` → `lda-entity-resolver` → `lead-scanner`) is the canonical
citation-first path from raw filings to leads; the semantic layer (`embed_corpus.py` /

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — Submission

> **Status: SKELETON.** This README is the submission map required by the challenge. Sections below
> match the six required elements verbatim; they are filled in as the work happens, not reconstructed
> at the deadline.

## What this is

An agentic investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional
press releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. Built as re-runnable Agent
Skills plus verified findings.

## Quick start (evaluators)

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --sample 2025-Q1   # smoke mode
python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>                    # view any cited record
```

Data layout under `data/` follows the challenge's data manual (`congress_press/`, `senate/`, `house/`).

## 1. Included skills

| Skill | What it does | How to run |
|---|---|---|
| `skills/lda-corpus-loader/` | Parses all three raw datasets into a queryable DB; every row keeps a raw-record pointer; includes `show_record.py` | `python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years YYYY ...]` |
| `skills/lda-entity-resolver/` | Cross-dataset entity table + alias map (Senate↔House crosswalk); resolves the `<senateID>` compound key, groups clients by normalized name (Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global) | `python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/resolve_entities.py --db db/lda.duckdb [--report]` |
| `skills/investigation-ledger/` | Lead/entity/thread tracking templates + schema lint | `python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md` |
| `skills/lead-scanner/` | (1) Lens library: SQL-first anomaly scans that emit candidate leads. (2) Bill cross-check: a bill number **or** named alias → every Senate/House filing + press release touching it. (3) LD-203 giving map: an entity/roster → its disclosed political giving. (4) Industry map: an industry hidden in the lobbying free-text → an entity-resolved player roster. (5) FEC enrichment: that roster → its Super-PAC contributions (openFEC), reconciled against LD-203 (the money LD-203 can't see) | Lenses: `python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX] [queries/<file>.sql]`. Bill: `python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Inflation Reduction Act"`. Giving: `lda_ld203_giving.py --names-file <roster>`. Industry: `lda_industry_map.py crypto`. FEC: `fec_enrich.py --names-file out/crypto_roster.txt` (needs `DATA_GOV_API_KEY`) |
| `skills/finding-verifier/` | Fresh-agent re-derivation of every claim in a finding before it locks | Protocol in `SKILL.md`, not yet automated end-to-end; delegates outside-source PDFs to `skills/source-document-reader` |
| `skills/source-document-reader/` | Turns an external primary-source PDF (court filing, SEC complaint) into page-anchored, citable text via render-to-image OCR that survives broken e-filing fonts; defines the external-document citation convention | `python skills/source-document-reader/scripts/ocr_pdf.py --pdf data/DOC.pdf --out data/DOC.ocr.txt [--grep TERM] [--dpi 300]` (needs the Tesseract engine — see `SKILL.md`) |
| `skills/outside-context-scan/` | Exploratory web research (novelty/prior-art + news-landscape checks) to triage a lead before drafting — distinct from finding-verifier's claim-locking rigor | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script |
| `skills/dataset-primer/` | Orients on an unfamiliar dataset before building against it: a bounded five-axis web scan (authoritative docs, tribal-knowledge gotchas, tooling, derived datasets, access mechanics) against a fixed nine-category data-quality checklist → a tiered, task-tailored reference brief cached in `reference/`. General-purpose research aid; sibling to outside-context-scan | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script. Example output: `reference/fec-campaign-finance.md` |
| `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` | R port of the [lobbyR](https://github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr) package running entirely against the local `data/senate/` corpus (no API key, no network): loads Senate quarterly filings with keyword/client/registrant/period/amount filters, then `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` remove double-counting before any spend totals; top-spenders-over-time and by-ALI-issue-code rollups; Shiny explorer at repo root (`dashboard_app.R`) | In R: `source("skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/scripts/local_senate_filings.R"); source(".../lobbyr_clean.R")` then `get_local_senate_filings(years = 2022:2026, issues = "...")` piped through `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()`. Dashboard: `shiny::runApp("dashboard_app.R")` from repo root. See its `SKILL.md` |

## 2. Which findings each skill supports

_Filled in as findings lock. See `findings/` (one file per locked finding)._

| Finding | Skills used | Trace files |
|---|---|---|
| — | — | — |

## 3. Where the relevant traces are

`traces/` — one JSONL per session, named `YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead-id>.jsonl`.
`traces/INDEX.md` maps finding → skill invocations → trace files → human decisions.
`traces/rendered/` — HTML renderings for fast review.
Human-judgment moments are logged in `DECISIONS.md` and cross-referenced from the index.

## 4. Outside data used

_Disclosed as used. Candidates: Congress.gov (bills/votes/committees), FEC (campaign finance),
FARA (foreign agents), Federal Register._

| Source | Used for | Where cited |
|---|---|---|
| openFEC API (`api.open.fec.gov/v1`, api.data.gov key; fetched 2026-07-07) — endpoints `/committees` (committee resolution), `/schedules/schedule_a` (itemized receipts), `/committee/{id}/totals` (published-total reconciliation) | L031 (crypto Enterprise-Map money leg): each crypto player's contributions into the Fairshake Super-PAC network (committees `C00835959` Fairshake, `C00836221` Defend American Jobs, `C00848440` Protect Progress), reconciled against LD-203 giving — the Super-PAC money LD-203 can't see | `skills/lead-scanner/fec_enrich.py` (SKILL.md "FEC enrichment"); LEDGER L031 row; raw responses cached in `out/fec_cache/` (gitignored; the cache + committee/transaction ids are the citeable form — FEC data is external, not in the DB). API key read from env/gitignored keyfile — never committed or traced |
| unitedstates/congress-legislators (`legislators-current.json` + `legislators-historical.json`, unitedstates.github.io, public domain CC0; fetched 2026-07-09) | Member resolution layer (P6): `members_all`/`member_terms` — every member serving 2021+, name parts/nicknames, party per term with dates, FEC candidate ids | `skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/build_members.py`; raw JSON cached in `out/congress_legislators_cache/` (gitignored) |
| FEC bulk committee files (`cm`/`ccl`/`cn` per cycle 2022/2024/2026, fec.gov bulk-downloads; fetched 2026-07-09) + openFEC `/committees?designation=D` (leadership-PAC sponsor ids; key from env, never committed) | Candidate-support committee crosswalk (P6): `member_committees` — campaign committees / leadership PACs / JFC participants, tier-labeled | `skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/build_members.py`; raw files cached in `out/fec_cache/bulk/` + `out/fec_cache/leadership_pacs/` (gitignored) |
| FARA bulk data — `FARA_All_ForeignPrincipals.csv` (efile.fara.gov/bulk, fetched 2026-07-06, updated daily by DOJ) | L006 (UAE sovereign wealth): confirming zero FARA registrations for the Mubadala family and that ADIA's six historical registrations all terminated by 2022 | `archive/LEDGER_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md` L006 row (pilot-scale ledger, archived 2026-07-06 — see CLAUDE.md); `queries/l006_mubadala.sql` header |
| SEC Litigation Release 26458 + complaint PDF; Tracxn team profile; innovairrs.com | L003 (Innovairrs identity link) — lead work, parked; allegations-only framing mandatory | `archive/LEDGER_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md` L003 row + Cold threads (pilot-scale ledger, archived 2026-07-06 — see CLAUDE.md) |

## 5. Conflicts of interest

_One statement per team member — connections (employment, financial, family, prior work) to any
person or organization named in the findings, or "none known against the entities named."_

| Team member | Statement |
|---|---|
| — | — |

## 6. Possible legal violations flagged

_Any finding whose `Legal flag:` field is not "none" is listed here for the evaluation panel._

| Finding | Flag | Provision |
|---|---|---|
| — | — | — |

General-purpose research aid; sibling to outside-context-scan | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script. Example output: `reference/fec-campaign-finance.md` |

| `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` | R port of the [lobbyR](https://github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr) package running entirely against the local `data/senate/` corpus (no API key, no network): loads Senate quarterly filings with keyword/client/registrant/period/amount filters, then `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` remove double-counting before any spend totals; top-spenders-over-time and by-ALI-issue-code rollups; Shiny explorer at repo root (`dashboard_app.R`) | In R: `source("skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/scripts/local_senate_filings.R"); source(".../lobbyr_clean.R")` then `get_local_senate_filings(years = 2022:2026, issues = "...")` piped through `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()`. Dashboard: `shiny::runApp("dashboard_app.R")` from repo root. See its `SKILL.md` |

WD=/private/tmp/claude-504/-Users-aarushisahejpal-Dropbox-northwestern-challenge/7a7f7c2e-8147-4b58-ae29-500d7b0ea3d1/scratchpad/nw-team
cd "$WD"
Rscript -e '
suppressPackageStartupMessages({source("skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/scripts/local_senate_filings.R"); source("skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/scripts/lobbyr_clean.R")})
tot_i <- 0; tot_e <- 0; rows <- 0
for (y in 2022:2026) {
  f <- tryCatch(get_local_senate_filings(years=y, client_name="Yuga", tidy_result=TRUE, ignore_disclaimer=TRUE), error=function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(f) || nrow(f)==0) next
  d <- flag_client_registrant_conflict(flag_dupes(f))
  tot_i <- tot_i + sum(as.numeric(d$income), na.rm=TRUE)
  tot_e <- tot_e + sum(as.numeric(d$expenses), na.rm=TRUE)
  rows <- rows + nrow(d)
  cat(sprintf("  %d: %d cleaned rows, income $%s\n", y, nrow(d), format(sum(as.numeric(d$income), na.rm=TRUE), big.mark=",")))
}
cat(sprintf("PATH B (lobbyR port, per-year): rows=%d  income=$%s  expenses=$%s  TOTAL=$%s\n",
  rows, format(tot_i, big.mark=","), format(tot_e, big.mark=","), format(tot_i+tot_e, big.mark=",")))
' 2>&1 | grep -vE "^Warning|masked|Attaching|doublecounting|documentation" | head -12

flag_dupes <- function(cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function, find_duplicates = TRUE, attempt_cleaning = TRUE) {
  if (find_duplicates) {
    dupes_flagged <- cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        registration_or_termination = stringr::str_detect(filing_type, stringr::regex("RR$|T$", ignore_case = TRUE)),
        quarter_number = stringr::str_extract(filing_type, "\\d+") |> as.integer(),
        is_amendment = stringr::str_detect(filing_type, "\\d+A$")
      ) |>
      dplyr::group_by(registrant.name, client.name, filing_year, quarter_number) |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        has_quarter = any(!is_amendment & !is.na(quarter_number)),
        has_amendment = any(is_amendment),
        registration_termination = any(registration_or_termination),
        is_duplicate = (duplicated(income) | duplicated(income, fromLast = TRUE) | duplicated(expenses) | duplicated(expenses, fromLast = TRUE)),
        checkme = dplyr::if_else(
          has_quarter & has_amendment, "CHECK",
          dplyr::if_else(!has_quarter & !has_amendment & !is_amendment, "CHECK",
                         dplyr::if_else(registration_termination | is_amendment | is_duplicate, "CHECK", "PASS CHECK"))
        )
      ) |>
      dplyr::ungroup()
  } else {
    dupes_flagged <- cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function
  }
  clean_attempt <- function(dataframe_with_flagged_dupes) {
    dataframe_with_flagged_dupes |>
      dplyr::filter(!registration_or_termination) |>

      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(dt_posted)) |>
      dplyr::slice_tail(n = 1) |>
      dplyr::ungroup()
  }
  if (attempt_cleaning) {

content.text:
Found it — **the $50K delta is a methodological difference, not an error, and it's traceable to a single filing.** This is exactly the material for the reproducibility paragraph:

## The reconciliation (Yuga Labs, total lobbying spend)

| Slice | DuckDB canonical view | Chris's lobbyR port | Agree? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2023 (Q2–Q4) | $200,000 | $200,000 | ✅ |
| 2024 (Q1–Q4) | $300,000 | $300,000 | ✅ |
| 2025 Q1 | $75,000 | $75,000 | ✅ |
| 2025 Q2 | **$50,000** | **excluded** | ⚠️ explained |
| **Total** | **$625,000** | $575,000 | |

The one divergent filing: Bracewell LLP's **"2nd Quarter – Termination"** report (posted 2025-07-21, $50,000). Chris's `flag_dupes(attempt_cleaning=TRUE)` **drops all termination filings by design** — a conservative choice, since terminations often duplicate a quarterly amount. But when the termination *is* the only report for that final quarter (as here), its real income is excluded. Rob's SQL view counts it as Q2's filing. Under LDA rules a termination report covers final-quarter activity, so $625K is the better number — which Dhrumil's PR *also* reached independently. **Three ways in, one explainable difference, every dollar tied to a filing.** That's a stronger reproducibility story than mere agreement — it proves the implementations are genuinely independent. Chris's termination behavior is his documented design, so per your rule: **not touched.**

## Two real bugs the test surfaced in Chris's loader (both currently unfixed)
1. **Multi-year loads crash** (`years=2022:2026`): `client.client_id` is integer in some years' JSON, character in others → `bind_rows` fails. Advertised feature, genuinely broken. ~2-line fix (coerce IDs to character before binding).
2. **2022 loads crash**: that year's JSON lacks `general_issue_code_display` (schema drift) → `unnest` fails.

Both were worked around per-year for the test, so nothing above depends on fixing them — but as shipped, an evaluator running `years=2022:2026` per his own SKILL.md hits a crash.

Also: the README "how it fits together" passage you asked for is written locally but **not yet pushed** — it describes the three layers + the lobbyR prior-art lineage (which Rob's SKILL.md itself credits, confirming your hunch).

Your call on two things:
1. **Push the README passage** as written?
2. **The two loader bugs** — apply minimal fixes myself (your "fix if broken" rule covers it), or leave them and flag to Chris? Given the July 15 deadline and that evaluators may run his documented multi-year example, I'd lean fix-minimally, noted clearly in the commit.

---
name: project_gain_challenge
description: GAIN Northwestern agentic-AI investigative journalism challenge; say-vs-pay Agent Skill
metadata: 
  node_type: memory

---

Aarushi is competing in the GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge (Northwestern),
building Claude Code **Agent Skills** over a federal corpus (2022–2026 Q1):
Senate LDA JSON, House LDA XML, and congressional press releases. Judged on
findings quality (gate) + four 0–3 axes: Organization, Corpus Efficiency, Human
Verifiability, Novel Capabilities; reproducibility is a gate (can't re-run → cap
1). Deadline was July 15 2026; prizes $5k/$2.5k/$1k. Multiple skills allowed;
skills must be MIT + open source, validate against agentskills.io/specification.

**Team SUBMISSION repo: `aarushisahejpal/northwestern-ai-challenge`** (not
djnf-26 — that's unrelated despite the local ~/Dropbox/northwestern-challenge
dir pointing at it). Rob Calvey's README/skills structure is the gold standard
(matches challenge format); combined 2026-07-14: Rob's main + Dhrumil's PR #4
(parallel loader, semantic layer in lda-corpus-loader/lead-scanner, lexicon
v1.4 w/ NFT phrases) + Chris Cioffi's lobbying-quarterly-filings R skill &
dashboard_app.R (text-analysis skill excluded per team decision). Full DB
pipeline: build_db.py → resolve_entities → build_members → lda_industry_map
--build-tags; tests in tests/ (p6 retrofit regression fails from upstream
FEC/legislator drift — pre-existing, not a bug). Optional 4.6GB embeddings DB:
dhrumil-public.s3 lda_full_2026-07-14.duckdb. PRs #7/8/9 (lexicon harvests
v1.5-1.7) still open against dev-dmil.

**Personal tinker repo (separate from the team repo):**
https://github.com/aarushisahejpal/say-vs-pay (private, `personal` remote),
local at `~/Dropbox/say-vs-pay`. Corpus lives at
`~/Dropbox/northwestern-challenge/data.zip` (+ `data_manual.md`).

**Built skill `say-vs-pay`:** semantic vector index (chromadb) over
lobbying-activity descriptions + passage-level press text; tools
extract_lobbying/extract_press/build_index/query/say_vs_pay/map_semantics/setup.
say_vs_pay surfaces cited "say vs pay" candidate pairs (bi-encoder retrieve →
optional cross-encoder rerank → bill/chamber tie-break) for the agent to
adjudicate — never auto-declares contradictions. Precision fixes (passage
embedding + cross-encoder) killed the false positives found in testing.

**Ease/size architecture (as of this session):** `--backend auto` = use
sentence-transformers(+MPS, ~2370 txt/s) if torch installed, else Chroma ONNX
MiniLM (no torch, ~350MB base install, portable but ~45-slow on CPU). Phrase
DEDUP before embedding = 3.7x fewer embeddings (1.29M→350k unique). setup.py =
one-command extract+build; `--zip` brings own data; `--demo` builds tiny bundled
sample (assets/*.jsonl, 2.4MB) so anyone can install+explore in ~1min with NO
corpus/torch (PROVEN via fresh clone). paths.py auto-resolves --index via
.saypay.json (gitignored). Default indexes recent years (2025-26); --all-years
for full. Cross-encoder (rerank.py) is OPTIONAL (needs torch), degrades to
bi-encoder. IMPORTANT: USE_TF=0 baked into embedding.py/rerank.py avoids
TensorFlow AVX crash (exit 134). Corpus extracted at ~/nw-build/data (8.6G).

**Collaborator:** Dhrumil Mehta (co-authors semantic mapping with Aarushi) —
credited in LICENSE/README/SKILL.md. Planned companion skill `lda-tidy` ports
the **lobbyR** R package (github.com/cran/lobbyR, author Chris Cioffi; Aarushi
contributed) data-quality checks flag_dupes / flag_client_registrant_conflict —

produce findings/ + traces/ (verify leads at source before asserting).

content.text:
Everything's pushed and green. Here's the wrap-up, including how your team map changes one of my earlier calls.

## State of the submission repo (`northwestern-ai-challenge` @ `9b110cc`)
- **Rob's structure** intact as the standard; **Dhrumil's PR #4** merged (tested first); **Chris's skill + dashboard** imported side-by-side, README table + "how it fits together" passage added.
- All work preserved as authored; the only touches were mechanical: one stale test constant (Dhrumil's own verified numbers), two crash fixes in Chris's loader (multi-year `bind_rows` type clash from 2024's JSON; zero-match `unnest` crash). Verified his methodology unchanged: multi-year call reproduces per-year totals exactly, and the 409→331 stablecoin regression is identical pre/post fix.

## How the team map reframes the $50K question
Earlier I said DuckDB's **$625K** was "the better number" because a termination report covers final-quarter activity. But that was *my* technical read of LDA rules — and per your map, **the person whose judgment governs what a termination filing means is Chris**. His `flag_dupes` drops terminations *deliberately* (his code comments say the removal is imperfect and should be hand-checked); a reporter who handles these documents daily may know that termination amounts are unreliable or double-reported in practice. So the right framing for the findings doc isn't "R undercounts" — it's:

> *Two independent pipelines agree on every regular quarterly filing for Yuga Labs (2023: $200K, 2024: $300K, 2025-Q1: $75K). They diverge only on one termination filing (Bracewell LLP, 2025-Q2, $50K) — excluded by the R pipeline's conservative termination rule, counted by the SQL canonical view. **Which rule is right is a domain call → Chris arbitrates.***

That's a genuinely strong verifiability exhibit either way — it shows independent implementations, exact agreement where methods agree, and a divergence traceable to one named filing and one explicit rule.

## For the team (deadline is tomorrow, July 15)
1. **Ask Chris the termination question** — his answer becomes a documented methodology decision (DECISIONS.md) and the reconciliation becomes a findings-doc paragraph. I can draft it as soon as he rules.
2. **PRs #7/#8/#9** (Dhrumil's lexicon harvests) still open against `dev-dmil` — same test-then-merge treatment when ready.
3. **The findings docs remain the big gap** — the README's findings table is still skeleton, and findings quality is the challenge's gating criterion.

Want me to draft the cross-validation findings paragraph now (with both numbers presented and the open question flagged), so it's ready to finalize the moment Chris weighs in?

content.text:
Here are three copy-pasteable drafts, in your voice:

---

## → Chris

> Hey Chris! Big news — your lobbying-quarterly-filings skill + the Shiny dashboard are now in the team submission repo (`northwestern-ai-challenge`), listed in the README skills table with the lobbyR lineage credited. We left your methodology completely untouched — but heads up that I made two purely mechanical crash fixes to `local_senate_filings.R` so evaluators can run your documented examples: (1) multi-year calls like `years = 2022:2026` were crashing because 2024's JSON parses IDs as text while other years parse them as numbers — IDs are now normalized before combining; (2) filters that match zero filings (e.g. a client that didn't lobby that year) were erroring instead of returning an empty table. Verified before/after that your numbers are identical (2025 "stablecoin": 409 raw → 331 after your dedup + conflict cleaning, unchanged).
>
> One real domain question only you can answer: **should income on a termination filing count toward a client's spend total?** Concrete case: Yuga Labs. Your pipeline and our SQL pipeline agree on every regular quarterly filing (2023: $200K, 2024: $300K, 2025-Q1: $75K). The only divergence is Bracewell's "2nd Quarter – Termination" filing (posted 7/21/25, $50K) — your `flag_dupes` drops all terminations by design, the SQL view counts it as the Q2 report, so we get $575K vs $625K. From your reporting experience, are termination amounts trustworthy as final-quarter activity, or double-reported often enough that excluding them is right? Whatever you say becomes the documented team rule — and this reconciliation is going in the findings doc as our reproducibility exhibit, so your call decides the headline number.

---

## → Rob

> Rob — status on the combined repo: your README/skills structure is the gold standard and everything folded into it. Two merges on main: (1) Dhrumil's PR #4 (tested first — full from-scratch corpus build, your test suite green), which extends your loader and lead-scanner in place; (2) Chris's lobbyR-port R skill + Shiny dashboard, added side-by-side as `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` with a row in your skills table and a short "how the pieces fit together" note after it — turns out your `lda-entity-resolver` SKILL.md already credits his `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` as prior art, so the lineage was just made explicit.
>
> One thing for your radar, *not* caused by any merge: the p6 retrofit regression fails with 5 unexplained changes (4 healthcare, 1 aipac — Wesley Bell $5K→$10K) on pre-merge main too. It's upstream drift in the FEC/congress-legislators data since the audit CSVs were frozen. Worth a re-freeze of those CSVs when you get a minute so the acceptance suite is fully green for evaluators.

---

## → Dhrumil

> Dhrumil — PR #4 is merged 🎉. Before merging I rebuilt the full corpus from scratch to test it: parallel loader verified (141,332 press / 418,170 Senate / 409,640 House, sanity report clean), semantic CLI degrades gracefully without the embeddings DB, and your crypto update reproduced exactly as documented — all 9,768 prior filings retained, 61 NFT additions. One fixup pushed to your branch pre-merge (`180b858`): the pkg_acceptance CRYPTO guard still froze the v1.2 numbers while your lexicon shipped v1.4, so the constants now match your own verified 9,829 — flag if you'd have handled it differently.
>
> Still open: #7/#8/#9 (harvests v1.5–1.7) target `dev-dmil`, not main — want me to give them the same test-then-merge treatment, or are you still iterating? Also FYI the p6 retrofit "5 unexplained changes" isn't your PR — it reproduces on pre-merge main; it's FEC/legislators drift since the audit CSVs froze. And I haven't pulled the 4.6GB embeddings DB yet — will grab it before we run semantic sweeps for findings.

---

Notes on choices I made (tweak freely): I put the **CSV re-freeze** ask to Rob (his packages) and the **#7/8/9 decision** to Dhrumil (his stack); Chris's message leads with the good news before the fixes, and frames the termination question so his answer directly sets the published number. Want any of them shorter/longer, or a fourth message to the whole group summarizing where the submission stands against tomorrow's deadline?

>

content.content:
# Finding: The pipe-materials war — DIPRA's Q1 2026 spending spike

**Status:** independently verified, PASS (2026-07-06, second fresh-agent pass) — awaiting human lock decision (not yet logged in DECISIONS.md)
**Lead:** L010 (descends from L004)
**Legal flag:** none
**Date drafted:** 2026-07-05

## Headline

In Q1 2026, the Ductile Iron Pipe Research Association (DIPRA) nearly doubled its own
recent-quarter lobbying spend to push "materials provisions" and "domestic sourcing"
language favoring iron pipe into five separate FY26 appropriations bills, a TSCA
reauthorization, and the NDAA, deploying former U.S. Representative Martha Roby as a
named lobbyist — while McWane Inc., a fellow ductile-iron-pipe manufacturer, showed no
comparable increase in its own separate lobbying spend that quarter. DIPRA plus McWane's
flat spend together outspent the plastic/copper-pipe registrants found in a targeted
search roughly 2.7-to-1, though only Copper Development Association's share of that
total shows a clear textual connection to the same fight (see C5 caveat below). The only
public trace of the fight is an unrelated press release that happens to describe
"ductile iron pipes" as the material used in a bipartisan water-infrastructure grant —
not the underlying appropriations/TSCA fight itself.

## Claims

**C1 — DIPRA's Q1 2026 filing is a real spike, not a normal quarter.**
DIPRA (via registrant Bradley Arant Boult Cummings LLP) reported quarterly income of
$350,000 / $310,000 / $310,000 / $290,000 across Q1–Q4 2025 (2025 average: $315,000),
then $540,000 in Q1 2026 — +54% over its highest 2025 quarter, +86% over the immediately
preceding quarter (Q4 2025), +71% over its 2025 average.
- Citations: `4c7a4352-89e7-459b-8806-103281faa317` (2025 Q1, $350K),
  `aef5c0b9-8ecc-4112-9f7b-179d79160396` (2025 Q2, $310K),
  `d7728fe3-0f18-444c-8969-85cbcc52d41d` (2025 Q3, $310K),
  `5adf05eb-9e01-431d-916c-ece830b296e1` (2025 Q4, $290K),
  `7e61b5d2-5200-4d78-9499-9cce289c4994` (2026 Q1, $540K).
- Aggregate SQL: `queries/l010_pipe_war.sql#L010c`. DB rebuild:
  `build_db.py --data-root data/ --db db/lda_pilot.duckdb --years 2025 2026`.

**C2 — The spike is DIPRA-specific, not industry-wide.** McWane Inc., a fellow
ductile-iron-pipe manufacturer, shows no comparable increase in the same quarter: Balch
& Bingham LLP billed McWane a flat $90,000 every single quarter from Q1 2025 through Q1
...
5:**Legal flag:** none
Shell cwd was reset to /Users/aarushisahejpal/Dropbox/northwestern-challenge

content.content:
L010/L003 leads (pre-finding) 
13:| L004 kill + L010/L003 leads (pre-finding) | lead-scanner queries (l004/l010/l003 SQL) + show_record deep reads + ledger updates | 2026-07-05_deep-dive_L004

===== the two over-limit descriptions =====
--- dataset-primer ---
description: Bounded outward research to orient on an unfamiliar dataset before building against it — the "how does this data actually work, and what will silently corrupt my results?" scan. Given a dataset (FEC campaign finance, PACER/court dockets, CMS claims or Open Payments, a state open-data portal, any new API or bulk dump) and — ideally — the specific task you'll use it for, it runs a five-axis web scan (authoritative source + data dictionary; tribal-knowledge gotchas; open-source tooling; cleaned/derived datasets; access mechanics + licensing) against a fixed nine-category data-quality checklist (coverage boundaries; grain / double-counting; entity resolution; versioning / amendments; time semantics; denominators; suppression / privacy; authoritative-vs-derived; join keys / crosswalks) and emits a tiered, task-tailored reference brief. Two modes: first-contact (full brief, no task yet) and targeted (the traps for one specific operation). Checks reference/ for an existing brief first and refreshes it rather than re-researching from scratch; saves new briefs there. A starting orientation, NOT a verification gate — every trap it surfaces is a hypothesis to test against a real sample, not a fact to trust. Sibling to outside-context-scan (that scans whether a lead is already reported; this scans how a dataset works). Use when starting on a data source you haven't worked before, when a build is about to depend on data whose cleanliness you haven't vetted, or when onboarding someone to a dataset.
model: sonnet  # bounded web research; the brief is hypotheses-not-gospel — mid-tier is the right spend
--- lead-scanner ---
description: SQL-first tools for turning a lobbying/disclosure corpus into leads. (1) A lens library — say-vs-pay, revolving door, spend anomalies, cross-chamber discrepancies, contribution flows, foreign influence, disclosure gaps — run as scans that emit candidate lead rows with record IDs, never quoted record text. (2) A bill cross-check that, given a bill number OR a named alias ("Inflation Reduction Act", "Farm Bill"), returns every filing and press release touching it, each with a show_record.py-resolvable citation key. (3) A disclosed-giving map (LD-203) that, given a registrant entity or an industry roster, reports its disclosed political giving. (4) An industry map that finds an industry hidden in the lobbying free-text under many issue codes and vague categories, tags it with a curated vocabulary, and emits an entity-resolved player list that feeds the giving map and the canonical-spend view unchanged, plus a free-text discovery loop (FTS + keyness + semantic/embedding search) that proposes new vocabulary for human triage. (5) An external campaign-finance enrichment (FEC/openFEC) that takes that roster and reports each player's contributions into an industry's Super-PAC network, reconciled against the disclosed-giving map. (6) A quarterly turnover tracker that diffs a quarter against the corpus — declared terminations, new engagements, firm swaps, in-house moves, and a firm churn scoreboard — the recurring beat report. Corpus-specific facts (table names, dedup rules, citation keys, mirror sources, the termination signal) come from `reference/corpus-profile.md`; industry/bill vocabulary from the versioned JSON lexicons beside the scripts; external-dataset traps from the `reference/` briefs. Use when generating or refreshing the lead pipeline, running a bill-level "who's been quietly lobbying this?" check, building the who / how-much / who-they-give-to map of an industry, or reporting a quarter's representation turnover.
model: inherit  # deliberate: the bill cross-check gets pulled into live investigation turns; an override would re-model the rest of the calling turn — run sweep sessions cheap via /model at session start
