toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Vendored from https://github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr (flag_dupes.R,
# flag_client_registrant_conflict.R). Both functions operate purely on the tidy data
# frame shape get_filings()/get_local_senate_filings() produce (registrant.name,
# client.name, filing_type, filing_year, filing_period, income, expenses,
# dt_posted) -- no API calls inside either one.
#
# See references/data_quality_notes.md for the double-counting rationale.
#
# ONE DELIBERATE DEPARTURE FROM UPSTREAM: flag_client_registrant_conflict()'s entity-match
# normalization (see normalize_entity_name() below). Root-caused against the real corpus,
# not assumed -- see references/data_quality_notes.md's "Entity name-variant matching" section
# for the full trace (Business Roundtable's self-filed row has registrant.name = "THE
# BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC." but client.name = "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC" -- not identical even
# within its own filing, in any of 5 years on file -- so the self-lobbying check never fired
# for it under the original punctuation/case-only normalization).

#' Normalize an entity name for cross-filing matching
#'
#' Registrants and clients in this corpus refer to the same real organization with
#' inconsistent legal-suffix and filler-word usage across filings and even, in some cases,
#' within a single self-filed row's own registrant.name vs. client.name fields (verified:
#' "THE BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC." vs "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC", same registrant.id/client.id,
#' every year 2022-2026). Stripping punctuation and case (the original normalization) isn't
#' enough -- this also drops a small, deliberately conservative list of legal-entity
#' suffixes/filler words as whole tokens (not substrings), so it can't accidentally truncate a
#' real word (e.g. it won't touch "COALITION" or "GROUP" or "ASSOCIATION", which are often
#' load-bearing parts of an org's actual name, not filler).
#'
#' Validated two ways against the full local corpus (317k filings) before adoption: (1) the
#' known Business Roundtable spellings ("BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE", "THE BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE",
#' "THE BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC.", "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE (THE)", "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC")
#' all collapse to one key, while the four genuinely distinct regional Business Roundtables
#' (Gulf South/Ohio/Colorado/American Turkish) correctly do not; (2) applied to all 30,528
#' distinct client.name strings corpus-wide (1,880 groups, 4,017 raw names collapse), every
#' one of the highest-false-merge-risk groups (shortest resulting normalized name -- "2U",
#' "3M", "ATT", "GAP", "ARM", "FMC", "IAC", etc.) was hand-checked and found to be a genuine
#' same-entity suffix/punctuation variant, not an accidental merge of two unrelated orgs.
#'
#' Tokenizes on whitespace rather than using a `\\b`-anchored regex deliberately -- this
#' project's own tooling hit a real bug where an inline `Rscript -e` invocation silently
#' mangled `"\\b"` into a literal backspace character before R ever parsed it (confirmed via
#' `nchar("\\btest")` returning a value inconsistent with two literal characters), which made
#' every `\\b`-based match silently match nothing. Token-splitting sidesteps that class of bug
#' entirely regardless of which execution path calls this function.
#'
#' @param x Character vector of registrant or client names.
#' @returns Character vector, uppercased, punctuation removed, common legal-suffix/filler
#'   tokens dropped, whitespace squished.
normalize_entity_name <- function(x) {
  stopwords <- c("THE", "INC", "INCORPORATED", "LLC", "LLP", "LP", "LTD", "LIMITED",
                 "CORP", "CORPORATION", "CO", "COMPANY", "PLLC", "PC", "PLC")
  x <- stringr::str_to_upper(x)
  x <- stringr::str_remove_all(x, "[[:punct:]]")
  tokens <- stringr::str_split(x, "\\s+")
  vapply(tokens, function(t) {
    t <- t[!(t %in% stopwords) & nzchar(t)]
    paste(t, collapse = " ")
  }, character(1))
}

#' Flag and Clean Duplicate or Dubious Lobbying Filings
#'
#' @param cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function A data frame of filings (e.g. from get_filings() or get_local_senate_filings()).
#' @param find_duplicates Logical. If TRUE (default), flags dubious filings using regex/heuristics.
#' @param attempt_cleaning Logical. If TRUE (default), removes all but the latest filing per registrant-client-quarter group.
#' @returns A data frame with diagnostic columns added and (optionally) duplicates removed.
flag_dupes <- function(cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function, find_duplicates = TRUE, attempt_cleaning = TRUE) {
  if (find_duplicates) {
    dupes_flagged <- cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        registration_or_termination = stringr::str_detect(filing_type, stringr::regex("RR$|T$", ignore_case = TRUE)),
        quarter_number = stringr::str_extract(filing_type, "\\d+") |> as.integer(),
        is_amendment = stringr::str_detect(filing_type, "\\d+A$")
      ) |>
      dplyr::group_by(registrant.name, client.name, filing_year, quarter_number) |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        has_quarter = any(!is_amendment & !is.na(quarter_number)),
        has_amendment = any(is_amendment),
        registration_termination = any(registration_or_termination),
        is_duplicate = (duplicated(income) | duplicated(income, fromLast = TRUE) | duplicated(expenses) | duplicated(expenses, fromLast = TRUE)),
        checkme = dplyr::if_else(
          has_quarter & has_amendment, "CHECK",
          dplyr::if_else(!has_quarter & !has_amendment & !is_amendment, "CHECK",
                         dplyr::if_else(registration_termination | is_amendment | is_duplicate, "CHECK", "PASS CHECK"))
        )
      ) |>
      dplyr::ungroup()
  } else {
    dupes_flagged <- cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function
  }
  clean_attempt <- function(dataframe_with_flagged_dupes) {
    dataframe_with_flagged_dupes |>
      dplyr::filter(!registration_or_termination) |>
      dplyr::mutate(dt_posted = as.POSIXct(dt_posted)) |>
      dplyr::group_by(registrant.name, client.name, filing_year, filing_period) |>
      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(dt_posted)) |>
      dplyr::slice_tail(n = 1) |>
      dplyr::ungroup()
  }
  if (attempt_cleaning) {
    cleaned_and_flagged_dataframe <- clean_attempt(dupes_flagged)
  } else {
    cleaned_and_flagged_dataframe <- dupes_flagged
  }
  message("This function either removed or identified lobbying filings that, if left in, could lead to doublecounting of spending on lobbying. It is not perfect. Please see documentation on tips for fact-checking these by hand.")
  return(cleaned_and_flagged_dataframe)
}

#' Identify and Resolve Potential Double-Counting of Client/Registrant
#'
#' Entity matching uses `normalize_entity_name()` (strips legal-entity suffixes/filler
#' words, not just punctuation/case -- see that function's doc for why: verified against
#' real filings, e.g. Business Roundtable's own self-filed row has registrant.name "THE
#' BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC." vs. client.name "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC", which the original
#' punctuation/case-only normalization never recognized as the same entity, in any of the 5
#' years on file).
#'
#' @param dataframe_that_i_determine A data frame of filings (e.g. from get_filings() or get_local_senate_filings()).
#' @param flag_conflict Logical. If TRUE (default), flags filings where an entity appears as both registrant and client elsewhere.
#' @param clean_doublecounts Logical. If TRUE (default), removes filings likely to cause double-counting.
#' @returns A data frame with a `flag` column and, optionally, conflicting rows removed.
flag_client_registrant_conflict <- function(dataframe_that_i_determine, flag_conflict = TRUE, clean_doublecounts = TRUE) {
  if (flag_conflict) {
    dataframe_that_i_determine <- dataframe_that_i_determine |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        registrant_clean = normalize_entity_name(registrant.name),
        client_clean = normalize_entity_name(client.name)
      )
    self_lobbying_entities <- dataframe_that_i_determine |>
      dplyr::filter(registrant_clean == client_clean) |>
      dplyr::distinct(entity = registrant_clean)

    flagged_cases <- dataframe_that_i_determine |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        flag = dplyr::case_when(
          registrant_clean == client_clean ~ "Report of all entity's spending",
          client_clean %in% self_lobbying_entities$entity ~ "Likely part of separate entity's report",
          TRUE ~ "No entity's report detected"
        )
      )
  } else {
    flagged_cases <- dataframe_that_i_determine
  }
  remove_client_registrant_conflict <- function(dataframe_i_cleaned_above) {
    dataframe_i_cleaned_above |>
      dplyr::filter(flag != "Likely part of separate entity's report")
  }
  if (clean_doublecounts) {
    filtered_dataframe_with_flagged_conflict_col <- remove_client_registrant_conflict(flagged_cases)
  } else {
    filtered_dataframe_with_flagged_conflict_col <- flagged_cases
  }
  message("This function either removed or identified lobbying filings that, if left in, could lead to doublecounting of spending on lobbying. It is not perfect. Please see documentation on tips for fact-checking these by hand.")
  return(filtered_dataframe_with_flagged_conflict_col)
}

chris quick question:

should the income on a termination filing count toward a client's spend total?

Here's the case I found. We computed one client's total spend two ways as a cross-check -- your R pipeline vs Rob's SQL database -- using that Yuga Labs company from Dhrumil's PR, since it has a nice clean filing history. Pushed (`e0a891a`). The termination question is fully closed:

## What just landed
1. **The fix to Chris's pipeline** (with his sign-off, comment in the code pointing to the decision log): terminations now count, registrations still dropped, and the one-filing-per-quarter dedup makes double-counting structurally impossible.
2. **The DECISIONS.md entry** — this is a textbook "human judgment moment" for the judges: cross-check surfaced a $50K divergence → traced to one filing → the domain expert checked the primary source and ruled → rule implemented + verified in both pipelines. Exactly what the challenge wants traces to show.
3. **Verification on record:** Yuga = **$625,000 in both pipelines now** (the cross-check fully reconciles); CEMEX proves the both-exist case counts once; the stablecoin shift (+9 rows) is entirely termination-only quarters.

## One honest footnote for the team
Chris's ported finding (`chris_full_corpus_trends_2022-2026.md`) was computed under the *old* rule (terminations dropped). Its verification PASS still stands — the fresh agent verified the claims under the claim's own stated methodology — but if anyone re-runs his numbers now, totals will come out slightly higher. That's expected, directionally conservative (his published numbers understate), and consistent with the vintage caveat already in the verification block. No action needed unless someone wants to refresh his tables before lock.

And nice moment for the submission story: the R↔SQL cross-check didn't just *find* the discrepancy — it got resolved by the domain expert against the primary source and both pipelines now agree to the dollar. That's the reproducibility argument made real.

content.content:
########## PR #10 (Rob, member map) ##########
files: 2 +533/-2
## Summary
- New `skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_member_map.py` — the mirror of the P4 industry map. Give it a member of Congress ("Lindsey Graham", "AOC") and it scans every LD-203 giver corpus-wide whose recipient resolves to that member, then tags each giver with `industry_lexicon.json` facets: "who gives to this member, broken out by industry" instead of "who are this industry's players."
- Attribution boundary matches the existing money tools: a giver is only tagged with a facet if it also appears on the **client** side of a tagged filing (self-representing player, e.g. an in-house registrant or trade group). An outside multi-client firm that merely files for one tagged client shows up as an "also files for" context note, never as attributed giving to that client's industry.
- Ambiguous same-surname members are reported, never guessed (same discipline as `member_resolve.py`).
- `skills/lead-scanner/SKILL.md` updated: new "Member map" subsection, capability list entry, and frontmatter description (trimmed the closing sentence to stay under the 1024-char spec limit).

## Why
Requested to extend the industry-mapping tools so a specific member of Congress can be the query, not just an industry — same money-map discipline, reversed direction.

## Test plan
The real corpus DB was mid-rebuild during this work, so it hasn't been run against `db/lda_full.duckdb` yet. It was validated end-to-end against a small synthetic DuckDB fixture (schema-matched, not real data) to catch logic bugs before handoff — confirmed: LD-203 amendment dedup still holds, the attribution-boundary case renders correctly (outside firm excluded from the facet, shown as context only), ambiguous names are reported not guessed, `--facet`/`--json` both work, and the `AOC` shorthand resolves correctly. A real id/tag mismatch bug was caught and fixed this way (facet `id` like `"critical-minerals"` differs from its `tag` like `"CRITMIN"`).

- [ ] Run `python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_member_map.py --db db/lda_full.duckdb` on a few real members and eyeball the totals against `lda_ld203_giving.py`'s existing member rollup for consistency
- [ ] Spot-check a few sample record keys via `show_record.py`
- [ ] Once satisfied, add the citeable `queries/p4b_member_map.sql` form (noted as a TODO in the script's docstring) using real resolved names from a `--json` run

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

skills/lead-scanner/SKILL.md
skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_member_map.py

content.content:
########## PR #11 (Dhrumil, prebuilt DB) ##########
files: 5 +36/-4
## What

The built `db/lda_full.duckdb` — 2022–2026 full corpus, entity/member layers and the 388K-vector semantic layer all included — is now hosted for direct download:

```
https://dhrumil-public.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/gain-investigation/lda_full.duckdb
```

Anyone evaluating or working on the investigation can `curl` that file into `db/` and start querying immediately — no raw-corpus download, no `build_db.py` run, and no GPU embedding pass. The URL is documented where someone would otherwise start building:

- **README quick start** — a `curl` one-liner right after the build commands
- **CLAUDE.md** — corpus-versions list, on the `db/lda_full.duckdb` entry
- **loader SKILL.md** — the semantic-layer section, as the alternative to `embed_corpus.py`

Verified live (HTTP 200, uploaded 2026-07-15 from the same day's full rebuild).

## Also: pinned deps for those who *do* build embeddings locally

A small `requirements-embed.txt` pins the opt-in embedding deps (`torch==2.13.0`, `sentence-transformers==5.6.0`, `pyarrow==25.0.0`, `einops==0.8.2` — einops is needed by the nomic model's remote code and previously only surfaced as a runtime ImportError). They stay out of the base `requirements.txt`; nothing but `embed_corpus.py` / `lda_semantic_search.py` uses them.

attachment.snippet:
1	# Vendored from https://github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr (flag_dupes.R,
2	# flag_client_registrant_conflict.R). Both functions operate purely on the tidy data
3	# frame shape get_filings()/get_local_senate_filings() produce (registrant.name,
4	# client.name, filing_type, filing_year, filing_period, income, expenses,
5	# dt_posted) -- no API calls inside either one.
6	#
7	# See references/data_quality_notes.md for the double-counting rationale.
8	#
9	# ONE DELIBERATE DEPARTURE FROM UPSTREAM: flag_client_registrant_conflict()'s entity-match
10	# normalization (see normalize_entity_name() below). Root-caused against the real corpus,
11	# not assumed -- see references/data_quality_notes.md's "Entity name-variant matching" section
12	# for the full trace (Business Roundtable's self-filed row has registrant.name = "THE
13	# BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC." but client.name = "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC" -- not identical even
14	# within its own filing, in any of 5 years on file -- so the self-lobbying check never fired
15	# for it under the original punctuation/case-only normalization).
16	
17	#' Normalize an entity name for cross-filing matching
18	#'
19	#' Registrants and clients in this corpus refer to the same real organization with
20	#' inconsistent legal-suffix and filler-word usage across filings and even, in some cases,
21	#' within a single self-filed row's own registrant.name vs. client.name fields (verified:
22	#' "THE BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC." vs "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC", same registrant.id/client.id,
23	#' every year 2022-2026). Stripping punctuation and case (the original normalization) isn't
24	#' enough -- this also drops a small, deliberately conservative list of legal-entity
25	#' suffixes/filler words as whole tokens (not substrings), so it can't accidentally truncate a
26	#' real word (e.g. it won't touch "COALITION" or "GROUP" or "ASSOCIATION", which are often
27	#' load-bearing parts of an org's actual name, not filler).
28	#'
29	#' Validated two ways against the full local corpus (317k filings) before adoption: (1) the
30	#' known Business Roundtable spellings ("BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE", "THE BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE",
31	#' "THE BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC.", "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE (THE)", "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC")
32	#' all collapse to one key, while the four genuinely distinct regional Business Roundtables
33	#' (Gulf South/Ohio/Colorado/American Turkish) correctly do not; (2) applied to all 30,528
34	#' distinct client.name strings corpus-wide (1,880 groups, 4,017 raw names collapse), every
35	#' one of the highest-false-merge-risk groups (shortest resulting normalized name -- "2U",
36	#' "3M", "ATT", "GAP", "ARM", "FMC", "IAC", etc.) was hand-checked and found to be a genuine
37	#' same-entity suffix/punctuation variant, not an accidental merge of two unrelated orgs.
38	#'
39	#' Tokenizes on whitespace rather than using a `\\b`-anchored regex deliberately -- this
40	#' project's own tooling hit a real bug where an inline `Rscript -e` invocation silently
41	#' mangled `"\\b"` into a literal backspace character before R ever parsed it (confirmed via
42	#' `nchar("\\btest")` returning a value inconsistent with two literal characters), which made
43	#' every `\\b`-based match silently match nothing. Token-splitting sidesteps that class of bug
44	#' entirely regardless of which execution path calls this function.
45	#'
46	#' @param x Character vector of registrant or client names.
47	#' @returns Character vector, uppercased, punctuation removed, common legal-suffix/filler
48	#'   tokens dropped, whitespace squished.
49	normalize_entity_name <- function(x) {
50	  stopwords <- c("THE", "INC", "INCORPORATED", "LLC", "LLP", "LP", "LTD", "LIMITED",
51	                 "CORP", "CORPORATION", "CO", "COMPANY", "PLLC", "PC", "PLC")
52	  x <- stringr::str_to_upper(x)
53	  x <- stringr::str_remove_all(x, "[[:punct:]]")
54	  tokens <- stringr::str_split(x, "\\s+")
55	  vapply(tokens, function(t) {
56	    t <- t[!(t %in% stopwords) & nzchar(t)]
57	    paste(t, collapse = " ")
58	  }, character(1))
59	}
60	
61	#' Flag and Clean Duplicate or Dubious Lobbying Filings
62	#'
63	#' @param cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function A data frame of filings (e.g. from get_filings() or get_local_senate_filings()).
64	#' @param find_duplicates Logical. If TRUE (default), flags dubious filings using regex/heuristics.
65	#' @param attempt_cleaning Logical. If TRUE (default), removes all but the latest filing per registrant-client-quarter group.
66	#' @returns A data frame with diagnostic columns added and (optionally) duplicates removed.
67	flag_dupes <- function(cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function, find_duplicates = TRUE, attempt_cleaning = TRUE) {
68	  if (find_duplicates) {
69	    dupes_flagged <- cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function |>
70	      dplyr::mutate(
71	        registration_or_termination = stringr::str_detect(filing_type, stringr::regex("RR$|T$", ignore_case = TRUE)),
72	        quarter_number = stringr::str_extract(filing_type, "\\d+") |> as.integer(),
73	        is_amendment = stringr::str_detect(filing_type, "\\d+A$")
74	      ) |>
75	      dplyr::group_by(registrant.name, client.name, filing_year, quarter_number) |>
76	      dplyr::mutate(
77	        has_quarter = any(!is_amendment & !is.na(quarter_number)),
78	        has_amendment = any(is_amendment),
79	        registration_termination = any(registration_or_termination),
80	        is_duplicate = (duplicated(income) | duplicated(income, fromLast = TRUE) | duplicated(expenses) | duplicated(expenses, fromLast = TRUE)),
81	        checkme = dplyr::if_else(
82	          has_quarter & has_amendment, "CHECK",
83	          dplyr::if_else(!has_quarter & !has_amendment & !is_amendment, "CHECK",
84	                         dplyr::if_else(registration_termination | is_amendment | is_duplicate, "CHECK", "PASS CHECK"))
85	        )
86	      ) |>
87	      dplyr::ungroup()
88	  } else {
89	    dupes_flagged <- cleaned_dataframe_from_previous_function
90	  }
91	  clean_attempt <- function(dataframe_with_flagged_dupes) {
92	    dataframe_with_flagged_dupes |>
93	      dplyr::filter(!registration_or_termination) |>
94	      dplyr::mutate(dt_posted = as.POSIXct(dt_posted)) |>
95	      dplyr::group_by(registrant.name, client.name, filing_year, filing_period) |>
96	      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(dt_posted)) |>
97	      dplyr::slice_tail(n = 1) |>
98	      dplyr::ungroup()
99	  }
100	  if (attempt_cleaning) {
101	    cleaned_and_flagged_dataframe <- clean_attempt(dupes_flagged)
102	  } else {
103	    cleaned_and_flagged_dataframe <- dupes_flagged
104	  }
105	  message("This function either removed or identified lobbying filings that, if left in, could lead to doublecounting of spending on lobbying. It is not perfect. Please see documentation on tips for fact-checking these by hand.")
106	  return(cleaned_and_flagged_dataframe)
107	}
108	
109	#' Identify and Resolve Potential Double-Counting of Client/Registrant
110	#'
111	#' Entity matching uses `normalize_entity_name()` (strips legal-entity suffixes/filler
112	#' words, not just punctuation/case -- see that function's doc for why: verified against
113	#' real filings, e.g. Business Roundtable's own self-filed row has registrant.name "THE
114	#' BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE, INC." vs. client.name "BUSINESS ROUNDTABLE INC", which the original
115	#' punctuation/case-only normalization never recognized as the same entity, in any of the 5
116	#' years on file).
117	#'
118	#' @param dataframe_that_i_determine A data frame of filings (e.g. from get_filings() or get_local_senate_filings()).
119	#' @param flag_conflict Logical. If TRUE (default), flags filings where an entity appears as both registrant and client elsewhere.
120	#' @param clean_doublecounts Logical. If TRUE (default), removes filings likely to cause double-counting.
121	#' @returns A data frame with a `flag` column and, optionally, conflicting rows removed.
122	flag_client_registrant_conflict <- function(dataframe_that_i_determine, flag_conflict = TRUE, clean_doublecounts = TRUE) {
123	  if (flag_conflict) {
124	    dataframe_that_i_determine <- dataframe_that_i_determine |>
125	      dplyr::mutate(

1	---
2	name: lead-scanner
3	description: SQL-first tools for turning a lobbying/disclosure corpus into leads. (1) A lens library — say-vs-pay, revolving door, spend anomalies, cross-chamber discrepancies, contribution flows, foreign influence, disclosure gaps — emitting candidate leads as record IDs. (2) A bill cross-check: bill number or named alias → every filing and press release touching it, cited. (3) A disclosed-giving map (LD-203) for an entity, industry roster, or a member of Congress (Graham, AOC) — who gives to them, by industry. (4) An industry map: finds an industry hidden in the free-text, tags it from a curated versioned lexicon, emits an entity-resolved player roster; an FTS/keyness/semantic loop proposes new vocabulary for human triage. (5) FEC/openFEC Super-PAC enrichment of that roster, reconciled against LD-203. (6) A quarterly turnover tracker — declared terminations, new engagements, firm swaps, in-house moves, churn. Use when generating/refreshing leads, a bill check, an industry or member money map, or a turnover report.
4	model: inherit  # deliberate: the bill cross-check gets pulled into live investigation turns; an override would re-model the rest of the calling turn — run sweep sessions cheap via /model at session start
5	---
6	
7	# Lead Scanner
8	
9	Six capabilities, one discipline: extraction and filtering stay in SQL; the model only ranks and
10	phrases hypotheses; output is record IDs + one-liners, never quoted record text.
11	
12	1. **A lens library** — SQL-first scans that turn the corpus into candidate lead rows.
13	2. **A bill cross-check** (`scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py`) — a bill *number* or *named alias* → every
14	   filing and press release that touches it, each with a citation key.
15	3. **A disclosed-giving map** (`scripts/lda_ld203_giving.py`) — a registrant entity or roster → its
16	   disclosed political giving (totals, recipients, per-entity split).
17	4. **An industry map** (`scripts/lda_industry_map.py` + `scripts/lda_freetext_discovery.py` +
18	   `scripts/lda_semantic_search.py`) — find an industry hidden in the free-text, tag it
19	   deterministically, emit an entity-resolved player list; `scripts/lda_member_map.py` runs the
20	   mirror query — given a member of Congress, which industries give to them.
21	5. **An external campaign-finance enrichment** (`scripts/fec_enrich.py`) — take that roster and add
22	   the Super-PAC money leg from FEC/openFEC, reconciled against the disclosed-giving map.
23	6. **A quarterly turnover tracker** (`scripts/lda_turnover.py`) — diff a quarter against the corpus:
24	   declared terminations, new engagements, firm swaps, in-house moves, firm churn scoreboard.
25	
26	## How this skill is bound to a corpus
27	
28	The *method* is corpus-agnostic; the *facts* are not. Three layers hold the corpus-specific parts so
29	this SKILL.md never has to:
30	
31	- **`reference/corpus-profile.md`** — the binding layer. Table/view names, citation-key formats, the
32	  dedup key (`period_invariant_key`), the mirror-source rule (`mirror_sources` / `primary_for_dollars`),
33	  and attribution grain (`attribution_grain`). Every caveat below names the profile field that carries
34	  the fact. Point the tools at a new corpus by refilling that file.
35	- **The JSON lexicons beside the scripts** — `industry_lexicon.json`, `bill_aliases.json`: versioned,
36	  cited, user-updatable vocabularies. The "what the investigator has found" layer; each carries a
37	  `_meta.discipline` block. Grow them as you learn; don't restate them here.
38	- **The `reference/` dataset briefs** — external-dataset traps (e.g. `fec-campaign-finance.md` for the
39	  FEC leg), produced by `dataset-primer`.
40	
41	**Naming convention:** `lda_*` scripts require the built DuckDB (the LDA corpus); `fec_*` requires the
42	external openFEC API; untokened scripts are corpus-agnostic. A script's filename tells you what it
43	needs. Each corpus-bound script also declares its required profile fields in its header.
44	
45	Requires a DuckDB built by `lda-corpus-loader` (cross-dataset lenses also use `lda-entity-resolver`'s
46	entity tables). Raw records are opened only through `show_record.py` — never grep the raw corpus.
47	
48	**Where results go:** these tools *generate* leads; they don't record findings. Current runs live in
49	the LEDGER (the crypto industry-map + money legs are L029–L031). Generated rosters/intermediates go to
50	the gitignored repo-root `out/`, never into `skills/`.
51	
52	## Bill cross-check — `scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py`
53	
54	**The problem it solves.** Press releases and members name bills ("the Farm Bill", "NDAA", "Inflation
55	Reduction Act"); filings cite `H.R.`/`S.` numbers. Matching on number alone finds the money but misses
56	the messaging (and fabricates "lobbied but publicly silent" bills — the trap that killed L004);
57	matching on name alone finds the messaging but misses the money. This bridges both directions:
58	
59	- **Number side** — exact match on `bill_mentions` (numbers pre-extracted from filings + press at load).
60	- **Name side** — whole-word regex on press text (and, on request, filing free-text), driven by the
61	  alias crosswalk.
62	
63	A query by *number* also pulls the name-cited press; a query by *name* reaches the number-cited
64	filings. Every row carries a `show_record.py` key.
65	
66	```bash
67	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py HR5376
68	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Inflation Reduction Act"
69	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Farm Bill"   # phrase-primary; no stable number
70	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py --list-aliases
71	```
72	
73	Useful flags: `--dataset`, `--json`, `--scan-freetext`, `--top`/`--limit`. The alias crosswalk is
74	`scripts/bill_aliases.json`; its `_meta` explains the discipline (why ambiguous acronyms and reused
75	low numbers like `H.R.1` are phrase-primary, not number-matched). Add a bill by adding an entry there.
76	
77	### Reading the output — caveats
78	
79	- **Per-item dollars rank, they don't total.** A filing naming several bills attributes its whole
80	  income to each (`attribution_grain` = filing-level, profile §3). For exact client dollars, use the
81	  `canonical_spend_view` (profile §4). 
82	- **Never sum `mirror_sources`; read `primary_for_dollars`.** The same quarterly is filed with both
83	  chambers, so summing double-counts; one chamber is the mirror and under-counts recent quarters
84	  (profile §1, §3). Filings are amendment/duplicate-deduped on `period_invariant_key`.
85	- **Press counts are raw and vintage-sensitive.** The press corpus grows over its window (profile §1),
86	  and a bill's attention concentrates in its brief legislative moment — so only compare "loudness"
87	  across bills of the *same* vintage, and read the by-year facet. Use shares, not raw counts.
88	- Citeable aggregate form: `queries/p2_bill_crosscheck.sql` (`P2a`–`P2d`).
89	
90	## Disclosed-giving map — `scripts/lda_ld203_giving.py`
91	
92	**The problem it solves.** An industry map has two halves: *what it spends to lobby* (the
93	`canonical_spend_view`) and *who it gives money to*. This answers the second from the disclosed-giving
94	records: resolve an entity (or a whole roster) to its filer name(s), and report total disclosed giving,
95	a breakdown by contribution type, top recipients, and a per-entity split — every sample row carrying a
96	`show_record.py`-resolvable key.
97	
98	```bash
99	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_ld203_giving.py "coinbase"      # one entity
100	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_ld203_giving.py --names-file out/roster.txt  # a roster
101	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_ld203_giving.py "payward" --loose  # recover resolver-split variants
102	```
103	
104	Useful flags: `--type`, `--since`, `--exact`, `--top`/`--limit`, `--json`.
105	
106	### Reading the output — caveats
107	
108	- **Attribution boundary (do not overstate).** These reports are filed by *registrants*, never by

attachment.snippet:
1	# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — Submission
2	
3	## What this is
4	
5	An agentic investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional
6	press releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. Built as re-runnable Agent
7	Skills plus verified findings.
8	
9	## Quick start (evaluators)
10	
11	```bash
12	pip install -r requirements.txt
13	python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --sample 2025-Q1   # smoke mode
14	python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>                    # view any cited record
15	```
16	
17	Data layout under `data/` follows the challenge's data manual (`congress_press/`, `senate/`, `house/`).
18	
19	To skip the build entirely (including the GPU-heavy embedding step), download the prebuilt
20	full-corpus database — semantic layer included — and save it as `db/lda_full.duckdb`:
21	
22	```bash
23	curl -o db/lda_full.duckdb https://dhrumil-public.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/gain-investigation/lda_full.duckdb
24	```
25	
26	## 1. Included skills
27	
28	| Skill | What it does | How to run |
29	|---|---|---|
30	| `skills/lda-corpus-loader/` | Parses all three raw datasets into a queryable DB; every row keeps a raw-record pointer; includes `show_record.py` | `python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years YYYY ...]` |
31	| `skills/lda-entity-resolver/` | Cross-dataset entity table + alias map (Senate↔House crosswalk); resolves the `<senateID>` compound key, groups clients by normalized name (Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global) | `python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/resolve_entities.py --db db/lda.duckdb [--report]` |
32	| `skills/investigation-ledger/` | Lead/entity/thread tracking templates + schema lint | `python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md` |
33	| `skills/lead-scanner/` | (1) Lens library: SQL-first anomaly scans that emit candidate leads. (2) Bill cross-check: a bill number **or** named alias → every Senate/House filing + press release touching it. (3) LD-203 giving map: an entity/roster → its disclosed political giving. (4) Industry map: an industry hidden in the lobbying free-text → an entity-resolved player roster. (5) FEC enrichment: that roster → its Super-PAC contributions (openFEC), reconciled against LD-203 (the money LD-203 can't see) | Lenses: `python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX] [queries/<file>.sql]`. Bill: `python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Inflation Reduction Act"`. Giving: `lda_ld203_giving.py --names-file <roster>`. Industry: `lda_industry_map.py crypto`. FEC: `fec_enrich.py --names-file out/crypto_roster.txt` (needs `DATA_GOV_API_KEY`) |
34	| `skills/finding-verifier/` | Fresh-agent re-derivation of every claim in a finding before it locks | Protocol in `SKILL.md`, not yet automated end-to-end; delegates outside-source PDFs to `skills/source-document-reader` |
35	| `skills/source-document-reader/` | Turns an external primary-source PDF (court filing, SEC complaint) into page-anchored, citable text via render-to-image OCR that survives broken e-filing fonts; defines the external-document citation convention | `python skills/source-document-reader/scripts/ocr_pdf.py --pdf data/DOC.pdf --out data/DOC.ocr.txt [--grep TERM] [--dpi 300]` (needs the Tesseract engine — see `SKILL.md`) |
36	| `skills/outside-context-scan/` | Exploratory web research (novelty/prior-art + news-landscape checks) to triage a lead before drafting — distinct from finding-verifier's claim-locking rigor | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script |
37	| `skills/dataset-primer/` | Orients on an unfamiliar dataset before building against it: a bounded five-axis web scan (authoritative docs, tribal-knowledge gotchas, tooling, derived datasets, access mechanics) against a fixed nine-category data-quality checklist → a tiered, task-tailored reference brief cached in `reference/`. General-purpose research aid; sibling to outside-context-scan | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script. Example output: `reference/fec-campaign-finance.md` |
38	| `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` | R port of the [lobbyR](https://github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr) package running entirely against the local `data/senate/` corpus (no API key, no network): loads Senate quarterly filings with keyword/client/registrant/period/amount filters, then `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` remove double-counting before any spend totals; top-spenders-over-time and by-ALI-issue-code rollups; Shiny explorer at repo root (`dashboard_app.R`) | In R: `source("skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/scripts/local_senate_filings.R"); source(".../lobbyr_clean.R")` then `get_local_senate_filings(years = 2022:2026, issues = "...")` piped through `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()`. Dashboard: `shiny::runApp("dashboard_app.R")` from repo root. See its `SKILL.md` |
39	
40	**How the pieces fit together.** Everything runs on the same corpus, and the parts back
41	each other up. Two full analysis pipelines were built independently: a SQL one
42	(`lda-corpus-loader` → `lda-entity-resolver` → `lead-scanner`), which loads all three
43	datasets into a database and runs the lead scans, and an R one
44	(`lobbying-quarterly-filings`), which loads the Senate filings directly and produces
45	cleaned spend totals. Both apply the same core discipline — throw out amendments,
46	duplicates, and double-counted filings before adding up any dollars (the R functions
47	`flag_dupes()` / `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` came first; the SQL canonical-spend
48	view arrives at the same rules independently and credits them in its SKILL.md). Because
49	the two share no code, any headline number can be computed both ways as a built-in
50	fact-check — when they agree, that's two independent confirmations; when they differ, the
51	difference traces to a specific filing and a documented judgment call. On top of both sits
52	the semantic search layer (`embed_corpus.py` / `lda_semantic_search.py`), which finds
53	filings that keyword lists miss — it proposes new search terms for human vetting, but
54	findings always cite the deterministic keyword-and-record chain, never a similarity score.
55	
56	## 2. Which findings each skill supports
57	
58	_Filled in as findings lock. See `findings/` (one file per locked finding)._
59	
60	| Finding | Skills used | Trace files |
61	|---|---|---|
62	| `findings/L010_pipe_materials_war.md` — DIPRA's Q1-2026 spend spike pushing iron-pipe materials provisions (independently verified 2026-07-06, fresh-agent PASS; awaiting lock) | `lda-corpus-loader` (DB build + `show_record.py` citations), `lead-scanner` (L010 SQL lenses, `queries/l010_pipe_war.sql`), `finding-verifier` (two fresh-agent passes), `investigation-ledger` (lead L010, descends from L004) | `traces/2026-07-05_deep-dive_L004-L003.jsonl` (lead development; see `traces/INDEX.md`) |
63	| `findings/chris_full_corpus_trends_2022-2026.md` — full-corpus trends: AI's arrival on K Street (Anthropic/OpenAI/a16z $0→$3M each), tariff lobbying +215% and still climbing, Continental Strategy +6,600% (verified), the SAP America single-quarter outlier catch, the Business Roundtable double-counting trap (author-verified + fresh-agent PASS 8/8, 2026-07-14; hedges in the verification block) | `lobbying-quarterly-filings` (per-year loads + `flag_dupes()` + `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` + `normalize_entity_name()`; fix trace in its `references/data_quality_notes.md`) | authored in `ChrisCioffi/agentic_investigation` (session logs there predate this repo); LOC-Nation anomaly independently corroborated by leads L025/L027 (`traces/INDEX.md`) |
64	
65	## 3. Where the relevant traces are
66	
67	`traces/` — one JSONL per session, named `YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead-id>.jsonl`.
68	`traces/INDEX.md` maps finding → skill invocations → trace files → human decisions.
69	`traces/rendered/` — HTML renderings for fast review.

Everything is merged. Main now has all five workstreams: `4d18465`.

## What just landed (all tested before merge)
| PR | Result |
|---|---|
| **#10 Rob's member map** | Ran his own test-plan checklist on the real DB: Graham $1.57M/659 items, AOC resolves, ambiguous names report-don't-guess, record key opens a real LD-203 filing, description within spec → **MERGED** |
| **#11 Dhrumil's prebuilt DB** | URL live (HTTP 200, exactly 4.6 GB), pinned embed deps reviewed → **MERGED** — evaluators can now skip the entire build |
| **dev-dmil harvests v1.5–1.7** | Merged to main; rebuilt tags; CRYPTO filing set **unchanged** (9,829 — zero gained/lost), +12 citation rows from GENIUS identifiers; guard updated to v1.7 → **ALL CHECKS PASSED** |

## What's left for submission — now 100% human inputs, zero code
1. **5 COI one-liners** → README §5 (everyone)
2. **Trace exports** — sessions since 07-10 marked "pending export" in the INDEX, plus today's merge/verification sessions (whoever ran each)
3. **Two lock decisions** in DECISIONS.md — L010 and Chris's finding (both fresh-agent PASSED; any team member signs off)
4. **Your interaction log** — after locks
5. Optional polish, if time: Rob re-freezes the p6 audit CSVs (makes the suite fully green for evaluators); the §4 "gitignored" wording fix; Rob's own TODO (`queries/p4b_member_map.sql`); refreshing Chris's finding numbers under the new termination rule

content.text:
Done — all five COI rows say "None known against the entities named in the findings," pushed (`0867cd5`). I added one small footnote under the table noting the lobbyR tool lineage (you + Chris), since it's already disclosed in §1 — that way nobody can later say it was hidden, but it's correctly framed as tool authorship, not a conflict with any entity in the findings.

## What "trace exports" means — plain version

The challenge requires *"full logs of the model sessions"* — the actual transcripts of every Claude session that did the work. In this repo that's the `traces/` folder: **one `.jsonl` transcript file per work session** (e.g. `2026-07-05_deep-dive_L004-L003.jsonl`).

Here's the gap: the team kept an index (`traces/INDEX.md`) describing *every* session — but for all the sessions since July 10 (Rob's dashboard builds, the packager work, etc.), the index says **"(pending export)"**. Meaning: the session happened, its work is described, but **the transcript file itself was never copied into `traces/` and pushed**. Claude Code saves each session's transcript locally on whoever's machine ran it — so "exporting a trace" is literally: find your session's transcript file on your computer, copy it into `traces/` with the right name, push.

That's why it's per-person: **the files only exist on the machine that ran the session.** Rob's July 10–13 package sessions are on Rob's machine; Dhrumil's semantic-layer sessions on his; today's merge/verification sessions on yours.

And yes — this is the same thing as your "interaction logs." One task, two names: your interaction log = exporting the transcripts of *your* sessions (including this one). The asks to teammates are just "do the same for yours."

For the group chat:

> also — "trace exports": the judges need the actual Claude session transcripts, not just our index describing them. everything since 7/10 says "pending export" in traces/INDEX.md. the transcript files only exist on whoever's machine ran the session — so please find yours, drop them into traces/ with the date naming convention, and push. I'm doing mine.

message.content: based on the submission materials do we even need that. can we just drop that. and i can just have interaction logs lol

content.text:
Honest read of the requirements: **yes, you can drop the teammate-export ask** — with one small tweak to stay airtight. Here's the reasoning:

## What the challenge actually requires
*"Interaction traces (JSON or rendered pages) showing model sessions **with human judgment moments**"* — it's evidence that humans steered the agent, tied to the findings. It is **not** literally "every session anyone ever ran must be uploaded."

## What we already have vs. what's "pending"
- **L010 (finding #1):** its development trace **is already exported** (`2026-07-05_deep-dive_L004-L003.jsonl`) ✓
- **Chris's finding (#2):** the README row already honestly says his session logs live in his original repo ✓
- **Human judgment moments:** `DECISIONS.md` is full of them, including yesterday's termination ruling ✓
- **The "pending export" sessions (07-10→14):** those back the *review packages* (dashboards) — supporting analysis, **not the submitted findings**. Nice-to-have, not load-bearing.

- **Your interaction logs** cover the highest-value missing piece anyway: these sessions contain the merge QA, the R↔SQL cross-check, Chris's ruling being implemented, and the fresh-agent verification — the single best "human judgment moments" material in the whole project.

## The one tweak to make dropping it safe
The trace INDEX currently says "(pending export)" on a dozen rows — a judge reads that as *incomplete*. One honest sentence in README §3 reframes it as a scoping decision instead:

> Traces are exported in full for the submitted findings and the human-judgment decision log; sessions that produced supporting review packages (not submitted as findings) are documented descriptively in `traces/INDEX.md` without exported transcripts.

Want me to add that line and push? Then the trace story is: **findings fully traced + your logs + everything else documented-by-index** — defensible, honest, and nobody else has homework.

One flag while we're here: your logs become the export for the DECISIONS rows that say "this session (export pending)" — so when you do them, include today's session (the termination fix + verification one). That's the trace a judge would actually want to read.

content.text:
Yes — that works, and it's simpler. To be precise about what you'd be accepting:

**What's fine about it:**
- The submission already has real traces (7 session files + the INDEX) and both findings' trace stories are covered — L010's transcript is exported, Chris's provenance is documented. The requirement is satisfiable *today* without anyone else lifting a finger.
- Your interaction logs, added later, are the strongest material anyway (cross-check, Chris's ruling, verification) — they slot in whenever, since traces are purely additive files.

**The two things to keep in mind:**
1. **"Later" has to mean before the deadline** — the traces are one of the four required submission components, so your logs need to land before you actually submit, not after.
2. **The dozen "(pending export)" notes stay visible in the INDEX** — a picky judge might read that as unfinished. That's the honest state though, and your logs covering the recent sessions blunt most of it. If it nags you at submission time, it's a one-sentence fix then; nothing needs deciding now.

So: no action from me, no asks to teammates, you add your logs when you're ready. The only thing I'd put on your personal checklist for the logs: **include this session** — it's the one with the termination decision and both verification runs, which is exactly what judges want to see humans doing.

That closes every open item on my side. The repo is submission-ready pending: your logs, two lock sign-offs in DECISIONS.md, and hitting the submit form.

message.content: should we drop pending export?

content.text:
My honest advice: **don't just delete the words — that makes it worse, not better.** Here's the trap:

Each of those INDEX rows names a specific trace file (e.g. `2026-07-10_p3-turnover-build.jsonl`). Right now the row says *"(pending export)"* — an honest note that the file isn't there. If we strip the marker, the row **cites a file that doesn't exist with no explanation** — a judge who tries to open it now sees what looks like a false citation instead of a documented gap. In a submission scored on verifiability, a dangling reference is worse than an admitted one.

So the real options:

1. **Leave them** — honest, costs a slightly "unfinished" look. Zero effort.
2. **Reframe them** — change *"(pending export)"* to *"(not exported — supporting-package session, not a submitted finding)"* on those rows, plus the one-sentence note in README §3 saying traces are exported in full for the submitted findings and the decision log. Same honesty, but it reads as a **scoping decision** rather than unfinished homework. ~2 minutes of work.

I'd do **#2** — it's the difference between a judge thinking "they ran out of time" and "they drew a sensible line." But #1 is genuinely acceptable if you'd rather freeze the repo.

Want me to do the reframe and push?

1	# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — Submission
2	
3	## What this is
4	
5	An agentic investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional
6	press releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. Built as re-runnable Agent
7	Skills plus verified findings.
8	
9	## Quick start (evaluators)
10	
11	```bash
12	pip install -r requirements.txt
13	python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --sample 2025-Q1   # smoke mode
14	python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>                    # view any cited record
15	```
16	
17	Data layout under `data/` follows the challenge's data manual (`congress_press/`, `senate/`, `house/`).
18	
19	To skip the build entirely (including the GPU-heavy embedding step), download the prebuilt
20	full-corpus database — semantic layer included — and save it as `db/lda_full.duckdb`:
21	
22	```bash
23	curl -o db/lda_full.duckdb https://dhrumil-public.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/gain-investigation/lda_full.duckdb
24	```
25	
26	## 1. Included skills
27	
28	| Skill | What it does | How to run |
29	|---|---|---|
30	| `skills/lda-corpus-loader/` | Parses all three raw datasets into a queryable DB; every row keeps a raw-record pointer; includes `show_record.py` | `python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years YYYY ...]` |
31	| `skills/lda-entity-resolver/` | Cross-dataset entity table + alias map (Senate↔House crosswalk); resolves the `<senateID>` compound key, groups clients by normalized name (Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global) | `python skills/lda-entity-resolver/scripts/resolve_entities.py --db db/lda.duckdb [--report]` |
32	| `skills/investigation-ledger/` | Lead/entity/thread tracking templates + schema lint | `python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md` |
33	| `skills/lead-scanner/` | (1) Lens library: SQL-first anomaly scans that emit candidate leads. (2) Bill cross-check: a bill number **or** named alias → every Senate/House filing + press release touching it. (3) LD-203 giving map: an entity/roster → its disclosed political giving. (4) Industry map: an industry hidden in the lobbying free-text → an entity-resolved player roster. (5) FEC enrichment: that roster → its Super-PAC contributions (openFEC), reconciled against LD-203 (the money LD-203 can't see) | Lenses: `python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX] [queries/<file>.sql]`. Bill: `python skills/lead-scanner/scripts/lda_bill_lookup.py "Inflation Reduction Act"`. Giving: `lda_ld203_giving.py --names-file <roster>`. Industry: `lda_industry_map.py crypto`. FEC: `fec_enrich.py --names-file out/crypto_roster.txt` (needs `DATA_GOV_API_KEY`) |
34	| `skills/finding-verifier/` | Fresh-agent re-derivation of every claim in a finding before it locks | Protocol in `SKILL.md`, not yet automated end-to-end; delegates outside-source PDFs to `skills/source-document-reader` |
35	| `skills/source-document-reader/` | Turns an external primary-source PDF (court filing, SEC complaint) into page-anchored, citable text via render-to-image OCR that survives broken e-filing fonts; defines the external-document citation convention | `python skills/source-document-reader/scripts/ocr_pdf.py --pdf data/DOC.pdf --out data/DOC.ocr.txt [--grep TERM] [--dpi 300]` (needs the Tesseract engine — see `SKILL.md`) |
36	| `skills/outside-context-scan/` | Exploratory web research (novelty/prior-art + news-landscape checks) to triage a lead before drafting — distinct from finding-verifier's claim-locking rigor | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script |
37	| `skills/dataset-primer/` | Orients on an unfamiliar dataset before building against it: a bounded five-axis web scan (authoritative docs, tribal-knowledge gotchas, tooling, derived datasets, access mechanics) against a fixed nine-category data-quality checklist → a tiered, task-tailored reference brief cached in `reference/`. General-purpose research aid; sibling to outside-context-scan | Protocol in `SKILL.md`; uses WebSearch/WebFetch directly, no custom script. Example output: `reference/fec-campaign-finance.md` |
38	| `skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/` | R port of the [lobbyR](https://github.com/Lobbying-DisclosuRe/lobbyr) package running entirely against the local `data/senate/` corpus (no API key, no network): loads Senate quarterly filings with keyword/client/registrant/period/amount filters, then `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` remove double-counting before any spend totals; top-spenders-over-time and by-ALI-issue-code rollups; Shiny explorer at repo root (`dashboard_app.R`) | In R: `source("skills/lobbying-quarterly-filings/scripts/local_senate_filings.R"); source(".../lobbyr_clean.R")` then `get_local_senate_filings(years = 2022:2026, issues = "...")` piped through `flag_dupes()` and `flag_client_registrant_conflict()`. Dashboard: `shiny::runApp("dashboard_app.R")` from repo root. See its `SKILL.md` |
39	
40	**How the pieces fit together.** Everything runs on the same corpus, and the parts back
41	each other up. Two full analysis pipelines were built independently: a SQL one
42	(`lda-corpus-loader` → `lda-entity-resolver` → `lead-scanner`), which loads all three
43	datasets into a database and runs the lead scans, and an R one
44	(`lobbying-quarterly-filings`), which loads the Senate filings directly and produces
45	cleaned spend totals. Both apply the same core discipline — throw out amendments,
46	duplicates, and double-counted filings before adding up any dollars (the R functions
47	`flag_dupes()` / `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` came first; the SQL canonical-spend
48	view arrives at the same rules independently and credits them in its SKILL.md). Because
49	the two share no code, any headline number can be computed both ways as a built-in
50	fact-check — when they agree, that's two independent confirmations; when they differ, the
51	difference traces to a specific filing and a documented judgment call. On top of both sits
52	the semantic search layer (`embed_corpus.py` / `lda_semantic_search.py`), which finds
53	filings that keyword lists miss — it proposes new search terms for human vetting, but
54	findings always cite the deterministic keyword-and-record chain, never a similarity score.
55	
56	## 2. Which findings each skill supports
57	
58	_Filled in as findings lock. See `findings/` (one file per locked finding)._
59	
60	| Finding | Skills used | Trace files |
61	|---|---|---|
62	| `findings/L010_pipe_materials_war.md` — DIPRA's Q1-2026 spend spike pushing iron-pipe materials provisions (independently verified 2026-07-06, fresh-agent PASS; awaiting lock) | `lda-corpus-loader` (DB build + `show_record.py` citations), `lead-scanner` (L010 SQL lenses, `queries/l010_pipe_war.sql`), `finding-verifier` (two fresh-agent passes), `investigation-ledger` (lead L010, descends from L004) | `traces/2026-07-05_deep-dive_L004-L003.jsonl` (lead development; see `traces/INDEX.md`) |
63	| `findings/chris_full_corpus_trends_2022-2026.md` — full-corpus trends: AI's arrival on K Street (Anthropic/OpenAI/a16z $0→$3M each), tariff lobbying +215% and still climbing, Continental Strategy +6,600% (verified), the SAP America single-quarter outlier catch, the Business Roundtable double-counting trap (author-verified + fresh-agent PASS 8/8, 2026-07-14; hedges in the verification block) | `lobbying-quarterly-filings` (per-year loads + `flag_dupes()` + `flag_client_registrant_conflict()` + `normalize_entity_name()`; fix trace in its `references/data_quality_notes.md`) | authored in `ChrisCioffi/agentic_investigation` (session logs there predate this repo); LOC-Nation anomaly independently corroborated by leads L025/L027 (`traces/INDEX.md`) |
64	
65	## 3. Where the relevant traces are
66	
67	`traces/` — one JSONL per session, named `YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead-id>.jsonl`.
68	`traces/INDEX.md` maps finding → skill invocations → trace files → human decisions.
69	`traces/rendered/` — HTML renderings for fast review.

content.text:
Cleanup done, pushed (`bbc85c4`), and my slip fixed in the very next commit — full transparency in the history.

## What the cleanup changed
1. **"Repo map (where to look)" table** near the top of the README — 11 rows, each top-level thing explained in one line. This is the real fix for "a lot going on": an evaluator now knows in 20 seconds that `skills/` is the submission, `findings/` is the payoff, `traces/`+`DECISIONS.md` is the audit trail, and everything else is supporting material.
2. **4 superseded zips deleted** (~2.8 MB of stale snapshots nothing referenced). Every zip cited in the INDEX/LEDGER and every package's current zip is untouched.
3. **§4 wording fixed** — FEC caches correctly described as tracked-for-reproducibility.
4. *(Owned mistake, fixed same minute: my `git add -A` briefly committed a local build log + data symlink; both removed and gitignored in `bbc85c4`. The one incidental roster change was a single cosmetic entity-name normalization — kept.)*

## What I deliberately did NOT touch
`archive/`, LEDGER/DECISIONS, referenced zips, big CSVs, GitHub Pages files, `dashboard_app.R` at root — all load-bearing for either the Organization score or cross-references.

Smoke test still passes on the final state. The repo now reads: clean top level → map → skills → findings → receipts.

Remaining on your personal list: interaction logs into `traces/`, then the submission form. That's it.

content.text:
Good timing — and I know exactly where these live. Two artifacts matter most:

1. **This session's transcript** — Claude Code stores it locally as a JSONL; it contains the whole combination story (PR testing, cross-check, Chris's ruling, cleanup).
2. **The fresh-agent verification transcript** — the subagent that verified Chris's finding has its own JSONL; that's the `finding-verifier` protocol's own artifact.

One **important flag before anything gets committed to a public repo**: session transcripts include the system context injected into the conversation — which for your sessions includes your **personal memory index** (project notes, some of which are explicitly private) and your email. So the export needs a **redaction pass** before it touches the public repo. Let me locate the files and check sizes first:
